standards.standard_description: Ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, how questions about key details in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'who,' 'what,' 'where,' 'when,' and 'why' questions after reading together.

questions.prompt:
Mia woke up early on Saturday. She put on her boots and grabbed a basket. She walked to the garden and picked strawberries until the basket was full.

Where did Mia go?
questions.hint: Look at the third sentence — where did she walk to?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Leo wanted to play fetch with his dog, Biscuit. He looked all over the yard for a stick but could not find one, so he grabbed the fuzzy yellow tennis ball from his backpack instead. He threw it far, and Biscuit raced after it and brought it right back. What object did Leo use to play fetch with Biscuit?
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells you what Leo threw across the yard for Biscuit to bring back.

questions.prompt:
On the first day of second grade, Emma felt nervous. She didn't know anyone in her new class. At lunch, a girl named Priya sat next to her and said, "Want to share my crackers?"

Why was Emma nervous?
questions.hint: Look at the second sentence — what made Emma feel nervous?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Grandpa and Tomas worked all morning to build a little wooden birdhouse. Before they began, they had already painted the boards a bright blue color. As soon as the very last nail was hammered in, they carried the birdhouse outside and hung it on a tree in the backyard. What did Grandpa and Tomas do right after they finished building the birdhouse?
choices: They painted it with bright blue paint.
They hung it on a tree in the backyard.
They put it on the kitchen table.
questions.correct: They hung it on a tree in the backyard.
questions.hint: Look at the last sentence of the story to see what happened after they used the paint.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ava could not wait to play in her big soccer game on Saturday. But when she woke up that morning, dark clouds filled the sky and heavy rain poured down. Her coach called to say the field was too wet, so the game could not happen. Ava felt very disappointed. Why was Ava disappointed?
choices: Her dad wanted to play a different game.
Her favorite board game was broken.
The soccer game could not happen.
It was too sunny to play outside.
questions.correct: The soccer game could not happen.
questions.hint: Think about what happened right before Ava felt disappointed in the story.

questions.prompt:
Sam brought his umbrella to school, but the other kids all wore sunglasses and shorts. At recess, everyone ran outside to play on the dry grass. Sam sat alone by the door, holding his umbrella and looking up.

Why did Sam probably feel out of place?
choices: He got ready for a different kind of weather than the others
He was the only one who liked recess
He forgot his sunglasses at home
He did not want to play any games
questions.correct: He got ready for a different kind of weather than the others
questions.hint: Compare what Sam brought with what the other kids brought and wore. What kind of day did each one expect?

questions.prompt:
Grandpa set two plates on the table and poured two glasses of milk. He looked at the clock, then at the front door, and smiled. He warmed the cookies one more time so they would still be hot.

What is Grandpa most likely getting ready for?
choices: He is about to leave the house
Someone is coming to visit soon
He is cleaning the whole kitchen
He is going to bake new cookies
questions.correct: Someone is coming to visit soon
questions.hint: Add the clues together: two plates, two glasses, watching the door and clock. Who is the second plate for?

questions.prompt:
Nina wanted to surprise her mom with breakfast in bed. She carried the tray very slowly and held her breath as she passed her little brother's room. She only let out her breath once she reached her mom's door.

Why did Nina hold her breath near her brother's room?
choices: She was afraid of the dark hallway
She did not like the smell of the food
She did not want to wake her brother up
She was tired from carrying the heavy tray
questions.correct: She did not want to wake her brother up
questions.hint: Think about why someone would be extra quiet ONLY while passing a sleeping person's room during a surprise.

questions.prompt:
When Tomas opened his lunchbox, he found a note from Dad and a sandwich cut into the shape of a star. He grinned. Last week Tomas had told Dad that plain square sandwiches were boring.

What does this tell us about Tomas's dad?
choices: He wanted Tomas to share his lunch with friends
He always makes star-shaped food for everyone
He forgot to pack a real lunch that day
He listened to what Tomas said and tried to make him happy
questions.correct: He listened to what Tomas said and tried to make him happy
questions.hint: Connect what Tomas said last week to what Dad did today. Why the star shape now?

questions.prompt:
The class was going to the zoo, but Ms. Lopez kept looking out the window at the gray sky. She told everyone to bring their raincoats and asked the driver to wait ten more minutes. Finally she said, 'Let's go, but stay close.'

Why did Ms. Lopez ask the driver to wait ten more minutes?
choices: She was hoping the weather might get better
She wanted the students to eat first
She needed time to count all the students
She was waiting for another class to join
questions.correct: She was hoping the weather might get better
questions.hint: Notice what Ms. Lopez keeps watching, and why that might make her pause before a zoo trip.

standards.standard_description: Recount stories including fables and folktales and determine their central message, lesson, or moral.
standards.parent_tip: After reading a story, ask your child 'What lesson did the characters learn?'

questions.prompt:
The ant worked all summer storing food. The grasshopper played and sang. When winter came, the ant had plenty to eat, but the grasshopper had nothing.

What is the lesson of this story?
questions.hint: Think about what the ant did differently — why was the ant okay in winter?

questions.prompt:
The tortoise and the hare had a race. The hare ran fast but stopped to nap. The tortoise kept going slowly and steadily. The tortoise crossed the finish line first.

What is the moral of this story?
choices: Slow and steady wins the race
questions.correct: Slow and steady wins the race
questions.hint: The tortoise never stopped. The hare did. Who won?

questions.prompt:
Rosa wanted the biggest piece of cake. She pushed ahead of everyone in line. Her friends were upset and didn't want to sit with her. Rosa wished she had waited her turn.

What is the lesson of this story?
questions.hint: What happened when Rosa pushed ahead? How did her friends feel?

questions.prompt:
A lion was caught in a hunter's net. A tiny mouse chewed through the ropes and set the lion free. The lion had once let the mouse go instead of eating it.

What is the message of this story?
choices: Even small friends can be a big help
questions.correct: Even small friends can be a big help
questions.hint: Who saved the lion? Was the mouse big or small?

questions.prompt:
Ben bragged that he was the best speller in class. During the spelling bee, he didn't study and missed an easy word. His friend Kai, who had practiced every night, won.

What is the central message?
questions.hint: Compare what Ben did and what Kai did to prepare.

questions.prompt:
A crow was thirsty and found a jug with a little water at the bottom. Her beak could not reach it. She looked around, then dropped one pebble after another into the jug. Slowly the water rose higher and higher, until at last she could drink.

What is the lesson of this story?
choices: Thinking of a clever plan can solve a hard problem
It is bad to be thirsty on a hot day
Crows like to collect shiny pebbles
You should always share your water with others
questions.correct: Thinking of a clever plan can solve a hard problem
questions.hint: Look at HOW the crow finally got her drink, not just that she was thirsty. What did she do to fix her problem?

questions.prompt:
A little mouse once gnawed a big lion free from a hunter's net. Long before that, the lion had caught the mouse but let him go. The lion had laughed, 'How could someone so small ever help me?' Now the lion was quiet.

Why does the story end with the lion being quiet?
choices: He was angry that the mouse chewed the ropes
He learned that even small friends can help you
He was tired from being stuck in the net
He was still hungry and wanted to eat
questions.correct: He learned that even small friends can help you
questions.hint: Remember what the lion SAID earlier about the mouse. Why would he stop laughing now?

questions.prompt:
A dog carried a bone across a bridge. Below, he saw another dog in the water holding a bone that looked even bigger. He opened his mouth to grab that bone too. His own bone fell with a splash and sank. Only then did he see the water was smooth like a mirror.

What is the main lesson of this story?
choices: It is dangerous to swim in deep rivers
Wanting more can make you lose what you already have
Dogs should not walk across narrow bridges
questions.correct: Wanting more can make you lose what you already have
questions.hint: The 'other dog' was really the dog's own reflection. Think about what he gave up trying to get more.

questions.prompt:
The wind and the sun argued about who was stronger. They saw a man in a coat. 'Whoever makes him take off his coat wins,' said the sun. The wind blew hard and cold, but the man pulled his coat tighter. Then the sun shone warm and bright, and soon the man took his coat right off.

What does this story teach?
choices: Arguing with a friend is a waste of time
The sun is hotter than the wind is cold
You should always wear a coat outside
Being gentle can work better than being forceful
questions.correct: Being gentle can work better than being forceful
questions.hint: Compare HOW the wind tried and HOW the sun tried. Which way made the man cooperate?

questions.prompt:
Every day a boy cried 'Wolf!' just to watch the villagers run up the hill for nothing. He laughed each time. One day a real wolf came. The boy screamed 'Wolf!' again and again, but this time nobody came, and the sheep ran away.

Why didn't the villagers come the last time?
choices: They no longer believed the boy after his false alarms
They wanted the wolf to eat the sheep
They were too tired from running up the hill
They did not hear the boy shouting
questions.correct: They no longer believed the boy after his false alarms
questions.hint: Think about all the times before when the boy tricked them. How would that change what they thought when he shouted?

standards.standard_description: Describe how characters in a story respond to major events and challenges.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child how characters feel when something happens in a story and what they do about it.

questions.prompt:
Lily's sandcastle was knocked down by a big wave. She felt sad at first, but then she smiled and said, "I'll build an even bigger one!"

How did Lily respond to the challenge?
choices: She decided to build a bigger one
questions.correct: She decided to build a bigger one
questions.hint: What did Lily say after she smiled?

questions.prompt:
During the talent show, Jake forgot the words to his song. His face turned red. Then he took a deep breath and started over from the beginning.

How did Jake handle forgetting the words?
choices: He asked the teacher for help
He took a deep breath and started over
questions.correct: He took a deep breath and started over
questions.hint: What did Jake do after his face turned red?

questions.prompt:
When the power went out during the storm, Maya was scared. Her mom lit candles and they told funny stories. Soon Maya was laughing instead of being afraid.

How did Maya's feelings change?
choices: She went from scared to laughing
She stayed scared the whole time
questions.correct: She went from scared to laughing
questions.hint: How did Maya feel at the start? How did she feel at the end?

questions.prompt:
Sam's best friend moved to a different city. Sam was really sad. He asked his mom if they could write letters to each other. Now Sam and his friend are pen pals.

How did Sam respond when his friend moved away?
choices: He found a way to stay in touch
He was angry at his friend
questions.correct: He found a way to stay in touch
questions.hint: What idea did Sam have to solve the problem?

questions.prompt:
Nora's team lost the championship game. Some kids were upset, but Nora high-fived every player and said, "We played our best. I'm proud of us."

What does Nora's response tell us about her?
questions.hint: Think about what Nora did and said after losing. Was she kind?

questions.prompt:
When the class pet, a hamster named Nibbles, escaped, Maya froze and covered her eyes. Ben grabbed an empty shoebox, laid it on its side, and put a piece of carrot inside. Soon Nibbles wandered in to eat, and Ben gently closed the lid.

How did Ben respond to the problem differently from Maya?
choices: Ben made a plan to catch Nibbles, while Maya was too scared to move
Ben chased Nibbles around while Maya fed him carrots
Ben was scared, while Maya made the plan to catch Nibbles
Both Ben and Maya covered their eyes and waited
questions.correct: Ben made a plan to catch Nibbles, while Maya was too scared to move
questions.hint: Look at what each child DID. One child acted and one child stayed still. Match the right action to the right name.

questions.prompt:
Rosa practiced her jump-rope tricks every day for two weeks. On the day of the contest, she tripped on her very first jump. She picked up the rope, wiped her eyes, and got back in line to try again.

What does "wiped her eyes" most likely tell you about Rosa?
choices: She had a piece of the rope stuck in her eye
She felt like crying but chose to keep trying anyway
She was sleepy and wanted to go take a nap
She was glad that she had tripped on her jump
questions.correct: She felt like crying but chose to keep trying anyway
questions.hint: Think about WHY someone would wipe their eyes right after they tripped in front of everyone. Then look at what she does next.

questions.prompt:
At first, Sam did not want the new boy, Theo, to join his soccer team. Sam thought Theo would make them lose. But late in the game, Theo passed the ball right to Sam so he could kick in the winning goal. Sam ran over and gave Theo a big high five.

How did Sam's feelings about Theo change during the story?
choices: Sam felt the same about Theo the whole game and never changed
Sam was angry at Theo from the start all the way to the end
Sam was worried about Theo at first, then was glad he had joined
Sam liked Theo at first, then wished he would leave the team
questions.correct: Sam was worried about Theo at first, then was glad he had joined
questions.hint: Compare how Sam felt at the BEGINNING with how he acted at the END. Something happened in between that changed his mind.

questions.prompt:
The storm knocked branches all over Grandpa's yard. Instead of turning on cartoons like she usually did, Ellie put on her boots, went outside, and began stacking the branches into a neat pile. When Grandpa looked out the window, he smiled.

Why did Ellie most likely start stacking the branches?
choices: Someone told her she was not allowed to watch TV today
She was searching the yard for the cartoons she missed
She wanted to build a fort out of the branches to play in
She wanted to help Grandpa clean up his messy yard
questions.correct: She wanted to help Grandpa clean up his messy yard
questions.hint: The word 'instead' is a clue. She gave up something she likes to do. What was she trying to do for Grandpa?

questions.prompt:
Kwan's little sister knocked over his block tower right before he could put the last block on top. His hands squeezed into fists, and he opened his mouth to yell. Then he saw her happy little face. He let out a big breath and said, "Do you want to build a new one together?"

Which word best describes how Kwan chose to respond in the end?
questions.hint: Kwan almost got angry, but focus on the CHOICE he made at the very end. What kind of person stays calm and shares?

standards.standard_description: Describe how words and phrases supply rhythm and meaning in a story, poem, or song.
standards.parent_tip: Point out fun-sounding words when reading poems or songs together — ask why the author chose those words.

questions.prompt:
The rain came down, drip, drip, drop. It wouldn't quit, it wouldn't stop!

What do the words **drip, drip, drop** help you imagine?
questions.hint: Say 'drip, drip, drop' out loud — what does it sound like?

questions.prompt:
Swoosh went the broom across the floor. Swish, swish, swoosh — it swept some more!

The words **swoosh** and **swish** help the reader —
choices: See the color of the broom
questions.hint: These words copy the sounds a broom makes!

questions.prompt:
Silly Sally sat on a seal, singing songs by the sea.

What makes this sentence fun to say?
choices: Many words start with the same sound
questions.correct: Many words start with the same sound
questions.hint: Look at the first letter of most words. What do you notice?

questions.prompt:
The leaves danced in the wind, twirling and spinning to the ground.

The word **danced** makes the leaves seem —
questions.hint: When you dance, how do you move? Happy or sad?

questions.prompt:
Tick-tock, tick-tock, the clock on the wall. Tick-tock, tick-tock, it counts for us all.

Why does the author repeat **tick-tock**?
choices: Because they forgot what to write
To copy the sound and rhythm of a clock
questions.correct: To copy the sound and rhythm of a clock
questions.hint: What sound does a clock make? Does the poem sound like that?

questions.prompt:
The turtle plods. He plods and plods. Slow . . . slow . . . slow he goes.
The rabbit zips! Zip-zip! He's gone before your nose.

Why did the poet write **slow . . . slow . . . slow** with long dots between the words?
choices: To make the reading feel slow, like the turtle moving
To show the turtle is lost and can't find his way
To show the turtle is quietly sleeping
To count how many steps the turtle took
questions.correct: To make the reading feel slow, like the turtle moving
questions.hint: Read those three words out loud with the dots. What does the spacing do to how FAST you say them?

questions.prompt:
BOOM went the drum. BOOM-BOOM! The whole room shook.
Then . . . tap. tap. tap — soft as a little mouse's foot.

How do the sounds of the words CHANGE from the start of the poem to the end?
choices: From loud and big to soft and small
From slow to fast but still very loud
questions.correct: From loud and big to soft and small
questions.hint: Compare the words at the very beginning to the words at the very end. Which are big, which are tiny?

questions.prompt:
"Are we there? Are we there? Are we there yet?" asked Sam again and again.
Mom just smiled. "Not yet, not yet, not yet."

The writer repeats **Are we there?** three times to help you feel that Sam is —
choices: scared of the long, dark road
feeling impatient and can't wait to arrive
getting very sleepy in the car
questions.correct: feeling impatient and can't wait to arrive
questions.hint: The words aren't just asking a question. Why would someone say the SAME thing over and over on a trip?

questions.prompt:
The old gate did not just open. It CREAKED and GROANED like it was in pain.

Why did the writer say the gate **groaned like it was in pain**?
choices: To explain that the gate is brand new and shiny
To warn that someone standing nearby is hurt
To show the gate was just painted a bright new color
To make the old gate seem alive and give it a sad, creepy sound
questions.correct: To make the old gate seem alive and give it a sad, creepy sound
questions.hint: A gate can't really feel pain. So what does giving it a 'groan' do to the mood of the sentence?

questions.prompt:
A song has this beat:

Clap your hands and stomp your **feet**,
Move to the drum and feel the ______!

Which word best fills the blank so the song KEEPS its beat and still makes sense?
questions.hint: The missing word has to do two jobs: rhyme with 'feet' AND match a drum.

standards.standard_description: Describe the overall structure of a story, including how the beginning introduces the story and the ending concludes the action.
standards.parent_tip: After reading, ask 'How did the story start? What happened in the middle? How did it end?'

questions.prompt:
Once upon a time, a girl named Zara found a magic key. She used it to open a secret door in her attic. Behind the door was a land of talking animals. After many adventures, Zara returned home and locked the door.

Which part is the beginning of the story?
questions.hint: The beginning introduces who the story is about and what starts the adventure.

questions.prompt: In a story, what does the ending usually do?
choices: Wrap up the story and solve the problem
questions.correct: Wrap up the story and solve the problem
questions.hint: Think about what happens at the very end of most stories you've read.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are starting to read a brand new story. What is the main reason the author writes the beginning part?
choices: To tell you how the characters finally solve their big problem.
To show you all the exciting adventures that happen in the middle.
To introduce the main characters and where the story is happening.
To explain what lesson the characters learned at the very end.
questions.correct: To introduce the main characters and where the story is happening.
questions.hint: Think about what the very first pages of a story usually teach you about the world and people in it.

questions.prompt:
Mia lost her puppy in the park. She looked everywhere. Finally, she heard barking behind the slide and found her puppy playing with another dog.

How does the ending conclude the action?
questions.hint: The ending solves the problem. What was Mia's problem?

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate. Sam raced down the sidewalk after him. A friendly mail carrier stepped on Biscuit's leash to stop him. Sam hugged his dog and walked back home."
Which sentence starts the story by giving the problem the rest of the story has to solve?
choices: Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate.
Sam hugged his dog and walked back home.
Sam raced down the sidewalk after him.
A friendly mail carrier stepped on Biscuit's leash to stop him.
questions.correct: Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate.
questions.hint: The beginning sets up the trouble. Which sentence tells you what goes wrong that everyone then reacts to?

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Nina could not find her library book anywhere. She looked under her bed and inside her backpack. Then she saw that her little brother had used it to build a block tower. Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag."
Which sentence ends the story by finally settling the problem from the beginning?
choices: Nina could not find her library book anywhere.
Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag.
Then she saw that her little brother had used it to build a block tower.
She looked under her bed and inside her backpack.
questions.correct: Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag.
questions.hint: Finding WHERE the book is isn't the same as the story being over. Which sentence shows the trouble is truly fixed?

questions.prompt:
Read the story. It is missing its last sentence:
"Theo wanted to ride the big kids' roller coaster, but he was always too short. All summer he measured himself against the door frame. On his birthday, he stood next to the ride's sign and finally reached the line. ______"
Which sentence would best END this story?
choices: The roller coaster was painted a bright, shiny red.
Theo went back to measure himself against the door frame again.
Theo climbed aboard the roller coaster at last, grinning from ear to ear.
Theo had always been afraid of high places.
questions.correct: Theo climbed aboard the roller coaster at last, grinning from ear to ear.
questions.hint: A good ending finishes the goal the story started with. Don't pick a brand-new problem or a sentence that just adds a detail.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"One rainy morning, a lost kitten sat shivering on the Ruiz family's porch. Mr. Ruiz wrapped it in a warm towel right away. The kitten purred and fell fast asleep. The family named her Rainy and kept her forever."
What is the MAIN job of the first sentence?
choices: It explains where the kitten got the name Rainy.
It shows the kitten's problem being solved.
It tells the reader how the story ends.
It introduces a character and the problem the story will solve.
questions.correct: It introduces a character and the problem the story will solve.
questions.hint: The opening sentence's job is to set things up. Notice the name clue is really paid off at the END, not started here.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Grandpa gave Lea a tiny seed and promised it would surprise her. Lea planted it and watered it every single day. Weeks went by with nothing but plain brown dirt. Then one bright morning, a huge sunflower had bloomed."
Why does the sentence "Weeks went by with nothing but plain brown dirt" belong in the MIDDLE of the story instead of at the end?
choices: It builds up the problem but does not solve it yet.
It tells the reader how the whole story turns out.
It introduces Lea and Grandpa for the very first time.
It describes the setting before anything happens.
questions.correct: It builds up the problem but does not solve it yet.
questions.hint: An ending settles things. Ask yourself: after this sentence, is the reader still waiting to see what happens?

questions.prompt: Read the story. "Ben got a new puppy named Spot. Spot chewed up a shoe and ran in the yard. At the end of the day, Ben and Spot fell asleep together." What happens at the BEGINNING of the story?
choices: Ben got a new puppy named Spot
questions.correct: Ben got a new puppy named Spot
questions.hint: The beginning is the very FIRST thing that happens. Look at the first sentence.

questions.prompt: Read the story. "Mia lost her red hat. She looked all over the house. Finally, she found it under her bed." What happens at the END of the story?
choices: She found it under her bed
She looked all over the house
questions.correct: She found it under her bed
questions.hint: The ending is the LAST thing that happens. Look at the last sentence.

questions.prompt: In most stories, what does the BEGINNING do?
choices: It is the back cover of the book
It has no words at all
It introduces the characters and starts the story
questions.correct: It introduces the characters and starts the story
questions.hint: Think about what a story does FIRST, before anything else happens.

questions.prompt: Read the story. "Sam planted a seed. He gave it water every day. After many days, a big flower grew." Which sentence is the BEGINNING?
choices: He gave it water every day
questions.hint: The beginning starts the story. Which sentence comes first?

questions.prompt: A story starts by telling you about a boy and his kite. Then the kite flies away, and at the end the boy gets it back. Which part is the ENDING?
choices: The boy gets his kite back
The story tells about a boy and his kite
questions.correct: The boy gets his kite back
questions.hint: The ending wraps up the story. What happens LAST?

standards.standard_description: Acknowledge differences in the points of view of characters.
standards.parent_tip: While reading, ask 'How does this character feel about what happened? Does the other character feel the same way?'

questions.prompt:
Ana thought the roller coaster was amazing and wanted to ride again. Her brother Carlos shook his head and said, "No way, that was too scary!"

How are Ana's and Carlos's feelings different?
choices: Ana loved it but Carlos was scared
Carlos loved it but Ana didn't
questions.correct: Ana loved it but Carlos was scared
questions.hint: What did Ana say? What did Carlos say? Are they the same?

questions.prompt:
Mom said, "We should get a cat." Dad said, "I think a dog would be better." Jayden said, "Can we get both?"

Who has a different point of view from Mom?
questions.hint: Mom wants a cat. Does Dad agree? Does Jayden agree exactly?

questions.prompt:
The farmer was happy when it rained because his crops needed water. The children were sad because they couldn't play outside.

Why do the farmer and the children feel differently about the rain?
choices: Rain helps the farmer's crops but stops the children's play
questions.correct: Rain helps the farmer's crops but stops the children's play
questions.hint: Think about why rain is good for the farmer and not fun for the kids.

questions.prompt:
Ella wanted to stay up late to watch a movie. Her mom said it was a school night and bedtime was 8 o'clock.

How is Ella's point of view different from her mom's?
choices: Ella wants to stay up but mom wants her to go to bed
Ella wants to sleep but mom says no
Mom wants to watch the movie too
questions.correct: Ella wants to stay up but mom wants her to go to bed
questions.hint: What does Ella want? What does Mom want? Are they the same?

questions.prompt:
In the story, the Big Bad Wolf said, "I just wanted to borrow some sugar!" The Three Little Pigs said, "He tried to blow our houses down!"

How are the points of view different?
choices: The wolf says he was friendly but the pigs say he was dangerous
The wolf admits he was bad
questions.correct: The wolf says he was friendly but the pigs say he was dangerous
questions.hint: The wolf and the pigs tell different stories about the same event!

questions.prompt:
It started to rain. Maya grabbed her boots and ran outside to jump in the puddles. Her sister Nina sighed, curled up on the couch, and pulled a blanket over her legs.

How do Maya and Nina feel differently about the rain?
choices: Maya is excited about the rain, but Nina would rather stay cozy inside
Both girls wish the rain would stop soon
Both girls are excited to splash in the puddles
Nina wants to play outside, but Maya wants to nap
questions.correct: Maya is excited about the rain, but Nina would rather stay cozy inside
questions.hint: Neither girl says how she feels. Look at what each one DOES when it rains, and picture how she must feel.

questions.prompt:
The class was choosing a pet. "Let's get a turtle," said Sam. "They are quiet, and I love just watching them." "Yes, a turtle!" said Priya. "We can each take turns feeding it every single day."

Sam and Priya both want a turtle, but how are their points of view different?
choices: Neither one really wants a turtle
Sam wants a quiet pet to watch, but Priya wants a pet to take care of
Both of them want a pet they can feed every day
Sam wants to feed the turtle, but Priya wants to watch it
questions.correct: Sam wants a quiet pet to watch, but Priya wants a pet to take care of
questions.hint: They agree on WHAT to get, but each gives a different reason. Read what each child says they like about the turtle.

questions.prompt:
At the playground, Leo climbed to the top of the tall slide and shouted, "This is the best!" Behind him, Theo held the rail tight, looked down, and whispered, "Maybe I'll just walk back down the steps."

How is Theo's point of view about the slide different from Leo's?
choices: Theo thinks the slide is too short and boring
Both boys are too scared to go down the slide
Theo feels nervous about the slide, while Leo feels excited
Theo thinks the slide is fun, just like Leo does
questions.correct: Theo feels nervous about the slide, while Leo feels excited
questions.hint: Nobody says the words 'scared' or 'excited.' Notice how each boy speaks and what his body does at the top.

questions.prompt:
Snow fell all night. In the morning Ben ran to the window and cheered, "Snow means no school today!" Grandpa looked out at the long driveway, then reached for his shovel by the door. "Going to be a lot of work," he said.

Which sentence best shows that Ben and Grandpa see the snow differently?
choices: Ben grabs the shovel to help clear the driveway
Both Ben and Grandpa are happy about the snow
Grandpa is the one who is excited that school is closed
Ben thinks the snow is fun, but Grandpa thinks it means hard work
questions.correct: Ben thinks the snow is fun, but Grandpa thinks it means hard work
questions.hint: Think about why the SAME snow makes one person cheer and the other reach for a shovel.

questions.prompt:
The friends talked about the field trip. "I hope we go to the zoo," said Rosa. "I really want to see the lions." Kwan shook his head. "The science museum has way cooler stuff." Mia nodded at Kwan and added, "And it won't be so hot inside there."

Whose point of view is MOST different from the others'?
choices: Rosa, because she wants the zoo while Kwan and Mia want the museum
Mia, because she does not agree with anyone
Kwan, because he is the only one who picks the museum
Nobody has a different point of view
questions.correct: Rosa, because she wants the zoo while Kwan and Mia want the museum
questions.hint: Figure out who agrees with whom first. What does Mia's nod tell you about her side?

standards.standard_description: Use information from illustrations and words to understand characters, setting, or plot.
standards.parent_tip: When reading picture books, ask your child what the pictures tell us that the words don't.

questions.prompt: If a story says **Tomas felt happy** and the picture shows him with a big smile and jumping, what do the words AND picture together tell you?
questions.hint: The words say happy. The picture shows a big smile AND jumping. That's MORE than just happy!

questions.prompt: A story's words say: "It was a dark night." The picture shows rain, lightning, and bent trees. What can you tell about the setting?
questions.hint: The words say dark. The picture adds rain, lightning, and bent trees. Put them together!

questions.prompt: In a story, the picture shows a character named Lily sitting alone with her head down, while a big storm rages outside her window. Based on the picture, how is Lily most likely feeling?
choices: A little bit sad or scared
Ready to go outside and play
questions.correct: A little bit sad or scared
questions.hint: Look closely at what Lily is doing and what is happening outside her window in the picture.

questions.prompt: A story says "Maya walked into the room." The picture shows Maya with wide eyes, an open mouth, and a room full of balloons and presents. What is probably happening?
choices: Maya is at a surprise party
questions.correct: Maya is at a surprise party
questions.hint: The words just say she walked in. But the PICTURE shows you what she found!

questions.prompt: The text says: "The old man sat on the bench." The picture shows him feeding pigeons with a gentle smile and a cane beside him. What do you learn from the picture that the words don't tell you?
choices: He's feeding pigeons and seems kind
questions.correct: He's feeding pigeons and seems kind
questions.hint: The words say he sat. What EXTRA information does the picture give?

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast two or more versions of the same story by different authors or from different cultures.
standards.parent_tip: Read two versions of the same fairy tale and ask your child what's the same and what's different.

questions.prompt: In one Cinderella story, a girl wishes to go to a royal party, and a fairy godmother helps her get there. In another Cinderella story, a different girl also dreams of attending a royal party, and magic fish help her. What happens in BOTH of these Cinderella stories?
choices: A fairy godmother helps the girl.
A girl attends a royal party.
The girl finds a magic wand.
questions.correct: A girl attends a royal party.
questions.hint: Look closely at what both girls want to do and what event they both go to in the stories.

questions.prompt: In an American version of a folktale, the hero is a cowboy. In a Japanese version, the hero is a samurai. Both heroes save their village. What is DIFFERENT?
choices: The stories have a happy ending
questions.hint: What is the same? They both save a village. What changes between the two stories?

questions.prompt: Both 'The Three Little Pigs' and 'The True Story of the 3 Little Pigs!' tell about a wolf and three pigs. What is a *big difference* about the wolf in these two stories?
choices: In one story, the wolf only likes to eat vegetables, and in the other, he eats meat.
In one story, the wolf lives in a brick house, and in the other, he lives in a straw house.
In one story, the wolf is shown as a bad guy, but in the other, he tries to explain why he did things.
In one story, the wolf is a pet, and in the other, he is wild.
questions.correct: In one story, the wolf is shown as a bad guy, but in the other, he tries to explain why he did things.
questions.hint: Think about who is telling each story and if the wolf is trying to be bad or just having bad luck.

questions.prompt: Imagine you read two different versions of the same story. In the first version, the main character is saved by a friendly helper. In the second version, the main character figures out how to escape all by themselves. When you compare these two stories, what is the most important difference about the characters' experiences?
choices: The number of trees in the setting.
How the main character gets rescued or escapes.
The colors of the characters' clothes.
Whether the main character feels scared.
questions.correct: How the main character gets rescued or escapes.
questions.hint: To find the most important difference, think about the main events that change how the story turns out for the character.

questions.prompt: In one telling of a race story, a slow snail races a fast rabbit. The rabbit stops to nibble carrots, and the snail crawls past to win. In another telling, a slow turtle races a fast deer. The deer lies down for a long nap, and the turtle keeps going to win. What is the SAME in BOTH stories?
choices: The slow animal wins because it never stops.
The race takes place in a snowy forest.
The fast animal wins the race in the end.
The fast animal stops to eat carrots.
questions.correct: The slow animal wins because it never stops.
questions.hint: Look at WHY the slow animal wins in each story, then find the reason that is true for both.

questions.prompt: A story from a hot desert land tells of a lost boy who is carried home by a friendly camel. A story from a cold, snowy land tells the very same tale, but the boy is carried home by a friendly reindeer. Why do you think each author chose a different animal?
choices: Because the boy in each story asked for that animal.
Because each animal fits the land where the story is told.
Because both animals are able to fly.
Because a camel runs faster than a reindeer.
questions.correct: Because each animal fits the land where the story is told.
questions.hint: Match each animal to its place. Where would you really find a camel? Where would you find a reindeer?

questions.prompt: In one version of a lost-shoe story, a girl named Mei runs from a party and drops a red silk slipper, and a prince finds it. In another version, a girl named Ana leaves a dance and drops a glass shoe, and a king's son finds it. What part of the story is the SAME in both?
choices: The lost shoe is made of shiny glass.
The girl dances with the king's own son.
A girl loses a shoe and a boy finds it.
The girl who loses the shoe is named Mei.
questions.correct: A girl loses a shoe and a boy finds it.
questions.hint: Cross out the details that change between the two stories. What is left that stays true in both?

questions.prompt: One author tells of a big wolf who huffs and blows down houses while the pigs scream and run. Another author tells the same tale, but the wolf is gentle and only knocks to share a pot of warm soup, and the pigs smile and let him in. Even though the wolf is different, how are the two stories ALIKE?
choices: Both stories end with the pigs running away.
Both stories feel scary from start to end.
Both wolves blow the little houses down.
Both stories are about a wolf coming to the pigs' houses.
questions.correct: Both stories are about a wolf coming to the pigs' houses.
questions.hint: The wolf ACTS differently in each. Look past how he acts and ask what BOTH stories are about.

questions.prompt: Long ago, one group of people made up a story that the dark shapes on the moon are a rabbit resting there. Far away, another group made up a story that those same shapes are the face of a kind old man. How are these two moon stories alike?
choices: Both stories try to explain the shapes people see on the moon.
Both stories are about the same old man's face.
Both stories say the moon is really a rabbit.
Both stories say the moon is made of soft cheese.
questions.correct: Both stories try to explain the shapes people see on the moon.
questions.hint: The two groups name different things, but ask WHY each group made up a story about the moon at all.

standards.standard_description: By end of year, read and comprehend literature in the grades 2-3 text complexity band proficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to read a mix of stories and poems that challenge them just a little.

questions.prompt:
The wind howled through the trees as Sophie clutched her flashlight. She had promised to walk to the mailbox alone, and she wasn't going to break her promise. With each step, the shadows seemed to dance around her.

How is Sophie feeling?
questions.hint: She made a promise and kept going — that's brave. But the howling wind and shadows suggest she's also a little scared.

questions.prompt:
Jackson's painting looked nothing like the sunset he was trying to copy. He almost crumpled it up, but then his art teacher said, "Art doesn't have to be perfect — it just has to be yours."

What did Jackson learn?
choices: Art should be a perfect copy
questions.hint: What did the teacher tell him? Does art have to be perfect?

questions.prompt:
Every day, the old lighthouse keeper climbed 137 steps to light the lamp. **Ships depend on me,** he would say. Even in the worst storms, the light always shone.

What word BEST describes the lighthouse keeper?
questions.hint: He climbed 137 steps every day, even in bad storms. That takes commitment!

questions.prompt:
The squirrel buried an acorn in the fall. By spring, a tiny green sprout poked through the soil. Years later, it became the tallest oak in the forest.

What is this story mainly about?
choices: Small things can grow into something big
questions.correct: Small things can grow into something big
questions.hint: Think about the journey — from a tiny acorn to the tallest oak tree.

questions.prompt:
"I'll never make friends here," whispered Amir on his first day at the new school. By the end of the week, he had joined the soccer team and made three new friends.

What happened that Amir did NOT expect?
choices: He went to a new school
questions.hint: What did Amir say at the beginning? Did that come true?

questions.prompt: Mia picked out the biggest slice of cake and put it on her own plate. Then she looked at her little brother Sam, who was staring at the empty tray. Mia sighed, cut her slice in two, and slid half onto Sam's plate. "There," she said quietly. What does Mia's sigh most likely tell us?
choices: She would rather have kept the whole big slice for herself
She was angry that Sam took her plate
She was tired from cutting the cake
She did not like the taste of the cake
questions.correct: She would rather have kept the whole big slice for herself
questions.hint: Think about what she wanted first, and what her sigh shows about how she felt while doing the kind thing.

questions.prompt: Every morning, old Mr. Diaz set a bowl of seeds on his windowsill, and the sparrows came to eat. One winter he was sick in bed for a week. When he finally opened the window again, only two sparrows came instead of the usual ten. Why did fewer sparrows most likely come back?
choices: Mr. Diaz put out a different kind of seed
Without food for a week, most of the birds found other places to eat
The sparrows did not like Mr. Diaz anymore
Sparrows only eat seeds in the summer
questions.correct: Without food for a week, most of the birds found other places to eat
questions.hint: Connect what stopped happening while he was sick to what the birds would need to do to survive.

questions.prompt: Ben told everyone he wasn't scared of the tall slide. But at the top, he held the railing tight and didn't move. "Go ahead!" called his friend. Ben looked down, then said, "I think I'll let you go first." Which sentence best shows that Ben was really scared, even though he said he wasn't?
choices: He held the railing tight and didn't move
He told everyone he wasn't scared
questions.correct: He held the railing tight and didn't move
questions.hint: Find the action that his body does, not the words he says. Actions can show a feeling words are hiding.

questions.prompt: Rosa planted a bean seed and checked it every single day. For a week, nothing happened, and she frowned each time. On Monday she almost forgot to look. When she did, a tiny green sprout had pushed through the dirt. Rosa clapped her hands. How did Rosa's feelings change from the beginning to the end of the story?
choices: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
questions.correct: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
questions.hint: Notice how she felt while she waited, and how she felt the moment something finally changed. Track both feelings.

questions.prompt: The two goats stood on a narrow log over the river. Neither would step back to let the other cross. They pushed and pushed until SPLASH — both goats fell into the cold water. What lesson does this story most likely teach?
choices: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
It is fun to push your friends
questions.correct: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
questions.hint: Think about WHY both goats ended up wet. What could they have done differently? The lesson is about their choice.

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, how questions about key details in a text.
standards.parent_tip: When reading nonfiction, pause and ask your child specific questions about who, what, where, when, and why.

questions.prompt:
Honeybees live in hives. A hive can have up to 60,000 bees! The queen bee lays all the eggs, and worker bees collect nectar from flowers.

What does the queen bee do?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — what is the queen bee's special job?

questions.prompt:
Firefighters wear special gear to stay safe. Their helmets protect their heads. Their thick coats keep them safe from fire and heat.

Why do firefighters wear thick coats?
choices: To keep safe from fire and heat
questions.correct: To keep safe from fire and heat
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you exactly why!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: George Washington Carver was a scientist who loved studying peanuts. He worked hard to find hundreds of new ways to use them. From peanuts, he made useful things for people, such as soap and paint that families could use in their homes. Which of these items did George Washington Carver create using peanuts?
questions.hint: The passage lists examples of what Carver made from peanuts. Look for that list.

questions.prompt:
Sea turtles travel thousands of miles through the ocean. They return to the same beach where they were born to lay their eggs.

Where do sea turtles lay their eggs?
choices: On the beach where they were born
In a nest in a tree
questions.correct: On the beach where they were born
questions.hint: Read the second sentence — which beach do they go back to?

questions.prompt:
In 1969, astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon for the first time. Millions of people watched on television.

When did astronauts first walk on the Moon?
questions.hint: The year is right at the beginning of the passage.

questions.prompt:
Every morning, Mrs. Lee puts a dish of seeds on her back porch. Soon little birds fly down to eat. But when her cat sits by the door, no birds come at all. So Mrs. Lee keeps the cat inside during breakfast time.

Why does Mrs. Lee keep the cat inside in the morning?
choices: So the birds are not scared away
So the porch stays nice and clean
So the cat can sleep a little longer
So the cat will not eat the seeds
questions.correct: So the birds are not scared away
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what happens when the cat is by the door. What does that clue tell you about why she moves the cat?

questions.prompt:
Sam plants a bean seed and watches it grow. First, a tiny root grows down into the soil. Next, a green stem pushes up. After many days, small leaves open at the top of the stem. Last of all, a flower appears.

When do the leaves open?
choices: At the very end, after the flower
After the stem grows but before the flower
Right after the root grows down
questions.correct: After the stem grows but before the flower
questions.hint: Put the four things in order. Then find where the leaves fit compared to the stem and the flower.

questions.prompt:
Ants are very strong for their size. When one ant finds a big crumb, it cannot carry the crumb alone. So the ant runs back to the nest and taps other ants with its feelers. Then many ants come and carry the crumb home together.

Why does the ant tap the other ants with its feelers?
choices: To warn them that danger is near
To show them the way back home
To ask them to help carry the crumb
To say hello to its friends
questions.correct: To ask them to help carry the crumb
questions.hint: Look at what happens right before and right after the tapping. What problem is the ant trying to solve?

questions.prompt:
Our class has three helpers each day. The line leader walks in front when the class moves. The door holder keeps the door open for everyone. The light monitor turns off the lights when the class leaves the room. Today, Maya is the very last one to do her job.

What is Maya's job today?
questions.hint: Which job happens LAST, when the class is already leaving the room?

questions.prompt:
Deep down in the sea, some fish make their own light. They live so far down that sunlight from above cannot reach them. Their glowing light helps them find food and see each other.

What can we tell about the deep sea from this text?
choices: It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
It has many green plants growing
It is warm and full of sunshine
It is right near the top of the water
questions.correct: It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
questions.hint: Why would a fish need to make its OWN light? Use that clue to picture what the deep sea is like.

standards.standard_description: Identify the main topic of a multiparagraph text as well as the focus of specific paragraphs.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child what a passage is mostly about, then have them tell you what each paragraph focuses on.

questions.prompt:
Dogs are very special animals that make great pets. They are often called "man's best friend" because they are so loyal and friendly. Dogs love to play with their families and often follow them around. Having a dog can make your home feel happier and safer.

There are many different kinds of dogs, both small and big. No matter their size, most dogs love to run and play outside. They enjoy going for walks and playing fetch with a ball. It's important to give dogs exercise so they stay healthy and happy.

Some dogs even have important jobs! For example, guide dogs help people who cannot see to walk safely. Police dogs work with officers to keep communities safe. Other dogs can help find people who are lost. These working dogs show how smart and helpful dogs can be.

What is the main topic of the text?
choices: Dogs need to exercise and play often.
Why dogs make wonderful pets and helpers.
How dogs help people with important jobs.
The different sizes and kinds of dogs.
questions.correct: Why dogs make wonderful pets and helpers.
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs in the text are mostly about together.

questions.prompt:
Paragraph 1: Apples come in many colors — red, green, and yellow. Paragraph 2: Apples are good for you because they have vitamins and fiber.

What is Paragraph 2 mostly about?
questions.hint: Paragraph 2 talks about vitamins and fiber. That means it's about health!

questions.prompt:
Read the passage below.

**Penguins!**

Penguins are amazing birds that live in very cold places, mostly in the Southern Hemisphere. You can find many of them in Antarctica, where it is snowy and icy. They love to be near the ocean because that is where they find their food. Even though they are birds, penguins cannot fly! They are excellent swimmers.

What do penguins eat? These busy birds are very good hunters in the water. They dive deep to catch tasty fish, tiny shrimp called krill, and other small sea animals. Penguins use their strong flippers to "fly" through the water as they chase their meals.

When it's time for baby penguins, the parents work together. The mother penguin lays one or two eggs. Both mom and dad take turns keeping the eggs warm until the chicks hatch. Once the babies are born, their parents feed them and protect them from the cold. Soon, the little penguins are big enough to swim and find their own food!

What is the MAIN topic of the whole passage?
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs teach you together.

questions.prompt:
Water is all around us, and it can look very different! Sometimes it's hard like a rock, and sometimes it flows like a river. Water can even disappear into the air. This is because water can change its shape or form.

One form of water is a solid. When water gets very cold, it freezes and turns into ice. Ice is solid water. You can hold ice in your hand, and it keeps its shape. Ice cubes in your drink and snow on the ground are examples of solid water.

Another form of water is a liquid. When ice melts, it becomes liquid water. This is the water you drink, use to wash your hands, or see in a pond. Liquid water flows and takes the shape of its container. It's the most common form of water we see every day.

The third form of water is a gas. When liquid water gets very hot, it turns into a gas called steam or water vapor. You can sometimes see steam rising from a hot pot of water. Water vapor is in the air all around us, even if we can't always see it. It helps make clouds!

Based on the entire text, what is the main topic?
choices: The three ways water can be found
questions.correct: The three ways water can be found
questions.hint: To find the main topic, think about what all the paragraphs are mostly talking about together.

questions.prompt: Read the passage below. What is the main topic of the passage? Many people love to have pets. Pets are animals that live with us in our homes. They can be fun and bring us joy. Dogs are popular pets. They like to play fetch and go for walks. Dogs need food, water, and lots of love. Cats are also common pets. They like to nap in sunny spots and chase toys. Cats need food, water, and a cozy place to sleep. Other pets include fish, birds, and hamsters. Fish swim in tanks. Birds sing songs. Hamsters run on wheels. No matter what kind, all pets need good care.
choices: How to take care of a cat.
Different types of animals at the zoo.
The many kinds of pets and how to care for them.
Why dogs are the best pets.
questions.correct: The many kinds of pets and how to care for them.
questions.hint: Think about what all the paragraphs are mostly about, not just one part.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Bees live together in a hive with one queen bee. Bees fly from flower to flower and use the sweet juice to make honey. When they land on flowers, they move yellow dust called pollen, and this helps new plants grow.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: The important jobs that bees do
How bees help new plants grow
Where the queen bee lives in the hive
How bees turn flower juice into honey
questions.correct: The important jobs that bees do
questions.hint: One choice covers the WHOLE text. The others are each true, but only tell about one small part.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Penguins cannot fly the way other birds do. But in the water they are amazing. Their wings work like flippers to push them fast, and they can dive deep down to catch fish.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Penguins cannot fly like other birds
The kinds of fish that penguins eat
questions.hint: The first sentence sets up a surprise. Read the whole paragraph to find what it spends the most time telling you.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Rain forests are hot, and it rains there almost every day. So many animals live in them, like tiny bright frogs and huge, long snakes. Rain forests also give people things we use, like fruit to eat and plants that become medicine.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: Why it rains so much in rain forests
The medicines that come from rain forest plants
What rain forests are like and why they matter
The huge snakes that live in rain forests
questions.correct: What rain forests are like and why they matter
questions.hint: A cool, exciting detail is not the same as the main topic. Ask what the text is about from start to finish.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Long ago, people had no clocks. They looked up at the sun to know the time. When the sun was high in the sky, it was the middle of the day. When the sun went down, they knew it was time to sleep.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Why people needed to build the first clocks
Why people feel sleepy at night
How the sun moves across the sky each day
How people long ago used the sun to tell time
questions.correct: How people long ago used the sun to tell time
questions.hint: Several choices mention something the paragraph touches. Find the one big idea that ties every sentence together.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

A librarian's day begins early. She turns on the lights and unlocks the big doors. All morning and afternoon she helps kids find books they will love. Before the library closes, she reads a story out loud so everyone can listen together.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: The different ways a librarian helps at the library
Why kids love to check out library books
How a librarian opens the library in the morning
How to find a good book on the shelf
questions.correct: The different ways a librarian helps at the library
questions.hint: Don't just grab the topic of the very first sentence. Notice what she does across the WHOLE day.

standards.standard_description: Describe the connection between a series of historical events, scientific ideas, or steps in technical procedures.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child see how events or ideas connect — ask 'What happened because of that?'

questions.prompt:
First, the caterpillar eats lots of leaves. Then, it makes a chrysalis. Finally, it comes out as a butterfly.

What happens AFTER the caterpillar makes a chrysalis?
questions.hint: Follow the steps: eat → chrysalis → what comes last?

questions.prompt:
In 1903, the Wright Brothers flew the first airplane. Their flight lasted only 12 seconds. Today, planes can fly across the whole world.

How are the events connected?
choices: The Wright Brothers' invention led to modern airplanes
The Wright Brothers flew across the world
questions.correct: The Wright Brothers' invention led to modern airplanes
questions.hint: The first event (1903 flight) started something big. What came from it?

questions.prompt: After you spread peanut butter on one slice, what is the main reason you put the other slice of bread on top?
choices: So the sandwich will have more crunch.
To keep the peanut butter from falling out.
To make the bread softer to chew.
Because it is the last thing you do.
questions.correct: To keep the peanut butter from falling out.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to the peanut butter after you spread it and before you put the other slice on top.

questions.prompt:
When bees visit flowers, pollen sticks to their legs. They carry the pollen to other flowers. This helps new plants grow.

How does pollen getting on bees connect to new plants growing?
choices: Bees carry pollen to other flowers, which helps plants grow
questions.correct: Bees carry pollen to other flowers, which helps plants grow
questions.hint: Follow the chain: pollen on legs → carried to flowers → what happens?

questions.prompt: The passage explains that the sun makes ocean water evaporate. What must happen to the water vapor *after* it rises for clouds to form?
choices: It must get much saltier to stick together.
It becomes colder and joins with other tiny pieces.
It needs to get hotter from the sun's strong rays.
It is pushed quickly across the sky by fast winds.
questions.correct: It becomes colder and joins with other tiny pieces.
questions.hint: Look for the part of the passage that describes what happens to the water vapor as it goes up high in the sky.

questions.prompt:
Sea otters eat sea urchins. When there are fewer sea otters, the number of sea urchins grows. As a result, the sea urchins eat too much kelp, and the kelp forests disappear.

What causes the kelp forests to disappear?
choices: Too many sea urchins eating the kelp
questions.correct: Too many sea urchins eating the kelp
questions.hint: Follow the chain: fewer otters means more urchins. What do the urchins do?

questions.prompt:
Clownfish live inside sea anemones. The anemone's stinging tentacles protect the clownfish from predators. In return, the clownfish cleans the anemone and brings it food.

How are the clownfish and anemone connected?
questions.hint: The anemone protects the clownfish, and the clownfish helps the anemone. That's a two-way connection!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: To make sea salt, workers first fill shallow pools with salty ocean water. Then they wait for hot, sunny days. The warm sun heats the water until it slowly disappears into the air, and only the salt is left behind in the bottom of the pool. When making sea salt, why is it important that the sun heats the ocean water?
choices: To make the water easier for workers to collect.
So the salt can become bright and shiny.
It causes the water to evaporate, leaving the salt behind.
To keep the shallow pools full of water.
questions.correct: It causes the water to evaporate, leaving the salt behind.
questions.hint: Look at what happens to the water right after the sun heats it, and what is left behind.

questions.prompt:
Plastic bags look like jellyfish in the water. Because of this, sea turtles sometimes eat the plastic by mistake. This can make them very sick.

Why do sea turtles eat plastic bags?
questions.hint: The passage says the bags LOOK like something turtles normally eat!

questions.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the shallow ocean near the shore help plants grow on the ocean floor?
choices: The shallow water helps small fish find the plants easily.
It lets sunlight reach the bottom of the ocean, where plants need it to grow.
The shallow water makes the plants grow big and strong.
It makes the water very cold for the plants.
questions.correct: It lets sunlight reach the bottom of the ocean, where plants need it to grow.
questions.hint: Look at what happens because the ocean near the shore is shallow, and then what that allows to happen for the plants.

questions.prompt:
A seed needs water to grow. When it rains, the ground gets wet, and the wet ground helps a seed sprout into a tiny plant. But when there is no rain for many weeks, the ground turns dry and hard, and the seeds just stay under the dirt.

Why do the seeds stay under the dirt when it does not rain for weeks?
choices: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
Birds carried the seeds away in the rain
The wet ground is too soft for them
The seeds are too old to grow anymore
questions.correct: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
questions.hint: Find the one thing the seeds need in the first sentence, then check what dry ground is missing.

questions.prompt:
To make a paper boat, Mia first folds the paper in half. Next, she folds the corners down to make a pointed top. Then she folds up the bottom edges to make the two sides. Last, she gently pulls the two sides open so the boat can float.

Why must Mia pull the sides open LAST and not first?
choices: The paper is easier to hold when it is flat
The sides must be folded before they can be opened
Pulling too early would make the paper wet
The corners cannot be folded once the boat is open
questions.correct: The sides must be folded before they can be opened
questions.hint: Look at which earlier step actually makes the sides. Can you open something that isn't there yet?

questions.prompt:
Long ago, people had to walk or ride horses to travel, and it took a long time. Then trains were built, and people could go much farther in less time. Later, cars were invented, so families could leave right from their own homes and travel whenever they wanted.

What did cars let people do that trains did NOT?
choices: Ride only along the train tracks
Travel before trains were even built
Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
Travel slower than a walking horse
questions.correct: Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
questions.hint: Compare the train sentence with the car sentence, and find the new thing only cars added.

questions.prompt:
Bees fly from flower to flower to drink sweet nectar. As a bee lands, yellow dust called pollen sticks to its fuzzy legs. When the bee flies to the next flower, some of that pollen rubs off. Flowers need pollen from other flowers before they can make new seeds.

Why are bees important to flowers?
choices: They dig the flowers' seeds down into the ground
They drink up all the nectar so the flowers stay dry
They eat the pollen off of every flower they land on
They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
questions.correct: They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
questions.hint: Follow the pollen: where does it stick, where does it go, and what do flowers need it for?

questions.prompt:
Sam planted a bean seed in a cup of dirt on Monday, and he watered it every single day. He did not see anything happen at first. Then on Friday, Sam noticed a tiny green stem poking up out of the dirt, so he kept watering it.

What most likely happened to the seed between Monday and Friday?
choices: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
It grew tall enough to open up its first flowers
It turned back into a bean for Sam to eat
It stayed exactly the same until Sam added new dirt
questions.correct: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
questions.hint: You see the start (a seed) and the end (a tiny stem). Think about what step must have happened in between, hidden in the dirt.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of words and phrases in a text relevant to a grade 2 topic or subject area.
standards.parent_tip: When your child encounters a new word, help them use the rest of the sentence to figure out what it means.

questions.prompt:
Nocturnal animals are active at night. Owls, bats, and raccoons are nocturnal.

What does **nocturnal** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence right after 'nocturnal' tells you — active at what time?

questions.prompt: Frogs often live near ponds and streams. These wet places are their habitats. What does the word **habitat** mean in this sentence?
choices: A type of food a frog eats
A natural home for an animal
A game a frog likes to play
A kind of weather frogs enjoy
questions.correct: A natural home for an animal
questions.hint: Look at where frogs live and what those places are called.

questions.prompt:
Mammals are warm-blooded animals that have fur or hair. Dogs, cats, and humans are all mammals.

Based on the text, what makes an animal a mammal?
choices: It is warm-blooded with fur or hair
questions.correct: It is warm-blooded with fur or hair
questions.hint: The first sentence describes exactly what a mammal is.

questions.prompt:
The ancient Egyptians built enormous pyramids. These huge stone structures still stand today.

What does **enormous** mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: The next sentence calls them 'huge stone structures.' That's a clue!

questions.prompt:
Plants need nutrients from the soil to grow. Nutrients are like food for plants — they give plants energy and help them stay healthy.

What are **nutrients**?
choices: Food-like substances that help plants grow
questions.correct: Food-like substances that help plants grow
questions.hint: The second sentence explains it — nutrients are like food for plants!

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about deserts:

"The desert gets almost no rain. To survive, a cactus stores water deep inside its thick stem. On the hottest days, when other plants wilt and die, the cactus stays green."

What does the word **stores** mean here?
questions.hint: Read the whole paragraph. Why would a cactus hold water inside its stem in a place with almost no rain? Use the clues about surviving the hottest days.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a science book:

"Bees are busy helpers in a garden. As a bee crawls into a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries this dust to the next flower. This is how bees pollinate the plants so new seeds can grow."

What does **pollinate** mean in this paragraph?
choices: Sting animals that come too close
Move flower dust so seeds can grow
Drink sweet juice from a flower
Build a home inside a flower
questions.correct: Move flower dust so seeds can grow
questions.hint: The word comes right after the bee carries dust from flower to flower. Match the meaning to what the bee is DOING, not just what it eats.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a report about the ocean:

"Deep in the sea it is pitch black. No sunlight reaches down that far. Yet some tiny fish make their own light. The glowing spots help them find each other in the dark."

What does **pitch black** mean here?
questions.hint: Look at the two sentences right after the phrase. What do they tell you about the light down there? Let those clues decide the meaning.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about weather:

"After days of rain, the little creek began to swell. The water climbed over its banks and spread across the road. Soon cars could not drive through."

What does the word **swell** mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: Don't guess from the word alone. Read what happens next to the water, then pick the meaning that fits that whole picture.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about the human body:

"When you run fast, your heart pumps harder. You breathe quickly, and your skin gets warm. Then your body sweats. The little drops of water cool you down so you don't get too hot."

What does the word **sweats** mean here?
choices: Makes drops of water to cool the body
questions.correct: Makes drops of water to cool the body
questions.hint: The sentence right after the word explains what the drops do. Use that clue instead of the other things the body does when you run.

standards.standard_description: Know and use various text features to locate key facts or information efficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Point out bold words, headings, and captions in nonfiction books and explain how they help readers.

questions.prompt: You want to find the meaning of the word **ecosystem** in a science book. Where should you look?
questions.hint: A glossary is like a mini-dictionary at the back of a book!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Maya opened a nonfiction book about the ocean. At the top of each part, she saw big, bold words like "Ocean Animals" and "Ocean Plants." Before she even started reading, those words told her exactly what each part of the book would be about. What is the purpose of a heading in a nonfiction text?
choices: To tell you what that section is about
questions.correct: To tell you what that section is about
questions.hint: Headings are like little titles for each section.

questions.prompt: You want to know which page has information about dolphins. Which text feature should you use?
questions.hint: An index lists topics alphabetically with their page numbers!

questions.prompt: In a nonfiction book, some words are in bold print. Why?
choices: To make the page look pretty
Because the author likes those words
To show important or new vocabulary words
questions.correct: To show important or new vocabulary words
questions.hint: Bold words stand out because they're important for the reader to know!

questions.prompt:
Here is the Table of Contents for a book about weather:

Chapter 1: What Is Weather? ... page 2
Chapter 2: Clouds and Rain ... page 8
Chapter 3: Storms ... page 14
Chapter 4: Weather Tools ... page 20

Maria wants to learn how a rain gauge measures how much water falls. Which chapter should she turn to first?
questions.hint: Think about WHAT a rain gauge is, not just the word 'rain.' Match the idea to the chapter, not the matching word.

questions.prompt:
Here is part of the Index from a bug book:

ants ... 4, 9
bees ... 7, 12
spiders ... 3, 15, 22
webs ... 15

Sam wants ONE page that tells about spiders AND their webs together. Which page should he open?
questions.hint: Look at the page numbers listed for 'spiders' and the page numbers listed for 'webs.' Find the number that shows up in BOTH lists.

questions.prompt: While reading, you see a word printed in dark, thick letters: **migrate**. You do not know what it means and want to find out as fast as you can. What is the BEST thing to do?
choices: Read the whole chapter over again from the start
Look at the pictures on the page and guess
Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
Look up 'migrate' in the index to see the page numbers it is on
questions.correct: Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
questions.hint: You want the MEANING of the word. Think about which text feature gives meanings, and remember what bold letters usually signal.

questions.prompt:
A page in a book has these headings:

- Where Sea Turtles Live
- What Sea Turtles Eat
- How Baby Sea Turtles Grow
- Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger

Max wants to find out how sea turtles catch the fish they hunt for food. Which section should he read?
choices: Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger
questions.hint: The heading may not use the exact words in the question. Decide what BIG idea 'catching fish for food' belongs to, then match it.

questions.prompt: A book about planets has BOTH a table of contents in the front and an index in the back. Rosa wants to find EVERY page that has the word 'Mars' on it. Which one will help her find all of those pages faster?
choices: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
The glossary, because it tells what the word 'Mars' means
The table of contents, because it lists the chapters in order
The title page, because it shows the name of the book
questions.correct: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
questions.hint: You want every single page where one exact word shows up. Think about which list is sorted by words, not by chapters.

standards.standard_description: Identify the main purpose of a text, including what the author wants to answer, explain, or describe.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'Why did the author write this? To teach us something, to tell a story, or to convince us of something?'

questions.prompt:
Brush your teeth twice a day. Use toothpaste with fluoride. Visit the dentist every six months.

What is the author's purpose?
choices: To explain how to take care of your teeth
questions.correct: To explain how to take care of your teeth
questions.hint: The author is giving you instructions about dental health.

questions.prompt:
The blue whale is the largest animal ever to live on Earth. It can be over 100 feet long and weigh as much as 200 tons.

The author wrote this to —
choices: Tell a story about a whale
Convince you to visit the ocean
questions.hint: Is the author giving you facts or telling a story?

questions.prompt:
Everyone should recycle! When we recycle, we save trees and keep trash out of the ocean. Please recycle your cans, bottles, and paper.

The author's main purpose is to —
choices: Teach you how paper is made
questions.hint: Words like 'should' and 'please' are clues that the author wants you to DO something.

questions.prompt: A book that teaches you about different types of clouds was written to —
questions.hint: If a book teaches you facts, the purpose is to inform!

questions.prompt:
Butterflies have four stages in their life: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, and butterfly. Each stage is an important part of their life cycle.

What question is the author trying to answer?
choices: What are the stages of a butterfly's life?
questions.correct: What are the stages of a butterfly's life?
questions.hint: The passage lists stages. What question does that answer?

questions.prompt:
Have you ever watched a spider fix its web? First, the spider makes one strong line between two branches. Then it adds more lines that spread out like the spokes of a wheel. Last, it spins a sticky circle to catch bugs. Now you know how a spider builds its web!

What is the author's MAIN purpose?
choices: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
To convince you that spiders are helpful
To describe what a spider looks like
To tell an exciting story about a spider and a bug
questions.correct: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
questions.hint: Look at how the text is built: first, then, last. What are those words showing you?

questions.prompt:
Why do leaves change color in the fall? In summer, leaves are full of green stuff that helps the tree make food. When the days get short and cold, that green stuff fades away. Then we can finally see the red and orange colors that were hiding all along.

The author wrote this text mostly to —
choices: Tell a story about a walk on a fall day
Answer why leaves change color in the fall
Explain how a tree makes its food in summer
Describe how pretty the red and orange leaves are
questions.correct: Answer why leaves change color in the fall
questions.hint: Two of the choices are true details from the text. Which one is the big question the whole text is answering?

questions.prompt:
Recess is the best part of the school day! Kids need time to run and play so their bodies stay strong. Playing outside also helps kids think better when they go back to class. Every school should give kids a long recess.

What is the author trying to do?
choices: Explain the rules of the games kids play at recess
Describe what a school playground looks like
Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
Inform readers about how the body stays strong
questions.correct: Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
questions.hint: Some sentences give facts, but notice words like 'best' and 'should.' What does the author want you to believe?

questions.prompt:
