The rainforest is loud and green. Tall trees block the sun, so the ground below stays dark and wet. Bright birds call from the high branches, and frogs sing near the little streams. Everywhere you look, something is moving.

The author mainly wants to —
choices: Convince readers to visit the rainforest
Explain why the trees grow so tall
Teach readers how to take care of frogs
Describe what the rainforest is like
questions.correct: Describe what the rainforest is like
questions.hint: Is the author giving you reasons WHY things happen, or painting a picture so you can imagine the place?

questions.prompt:
Some people think bats are birds because they fly. But bats are not birds at all — they are mammals, just like dogs and people. Bats have soft fur instead of feathers, and mother bats feed their babies milk.

Why did the author write this?
choices: To correct the idea that bats are birds
To describe what baby bats look like
To explain how dogs and people are alike
To tell readers where bats like to sleep
questions.correct: To correct the idea that bats are birds
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you a wrong idea some people have. What does the rest of the text do with that wrong idea?

standards.standard_description: Explain how specific images contribute to and clarify a text.
standards.parent_tip: When you see diagrams or photos in a book, ask your child what new information the picture adds.

questions.prompt: A science book has a paragraph about the parts of a plant. Next to it is a diagram with labels pointing to the roots, stem, leaves, and flower. How does the diagram help?
choices: It makes the page look nice
It shows you exactly where each plant part is
questions.correct: It shows you exactly where each plant part is
questions.hint: The paragraph tells you ABOUT the parts. The diagram shows you WHERE they are.

questions.prompt: A text about weather says: "A thermometer measures temperature." Below it is a picture of a thermometer showing 32°F. What does the picture add?
choices: It replaces the need to read
It shows what a thermometer looks like and how to read it
questions.correct: It shows what a thermometer looks like and how to read it
questions.hint: The words explain what it does. The picture shows what it LOOKS like and a reading.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In her book about animals around the world, the author added a large, colorful map. On the map, she placed small pictures of animals on top of the different countries where they are found. By looking at it, readers could see which animals lived in each part of the world. Why would an author include a map in a book about animals around the world?
choices: To show where different animals live
questions.correct: To show where different animals live
questions.hint: Maps show locations. If the book is about animals around the world, what would a map show?

questions.prompt: A book about healthy eating includes a picture of a food plate divided into sections: fruits, vegetables, grains, protein, and dairy. This image helps the reader —
choices: See how much of each food group to eat
Know what to buy at the store
questions.correct: See how much of each food group to eat
questions.hint: The plate shows sections of different sizes — what does that tell you?

questions.prompt: A book says: "A fox has a bushy tail." Next to the words is a photo of a fox. What does the photo help you do?
choices: See what a fox looks like
questions.correct: See what a fox looks like
questions.hint: The picture shows you the animal the words are talking about.

questions.prompt: A page about bugs has a photo of a ladybug with an arrow pointing to its red wings. What does the arrow do?
questions.hint: An arrow points to the exact part it wants you to notice.

questions.prompt: A book says: "This is how you tie a shoe." Below the words are step-by-step pictures of hands tying a shoe. How do the pictures help?
choices: They show each step of tying a shoe
questions.correct: They show each step of tying a shoe
questions.hint: Pictures can show you how to do each step, one at a time.

questions.prompt: A page about the sun has a big yellow picture of the sun. The words say the sun is bright and yellow. What does the picture add?
choices: It shows the sun's color and shape
questions.correct: It shows the sun's color and shape
questions.hint: Look at what the picture lets you SEE that the words describe.

questions.prompt: A book about a bike has a photo of a bike with a label that says "wheel" pointing to a wheel. What does the label help you learn?
questions.hint: A label names the part it points to so you know what it is.

standards.standard_description: Describe how reasons support specific points the author makes in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'What reasons does the author give?' after reading a persuasive or explanatory text.

questions.prompt: The author says recess helps kids "focus better in class." How does this reason help you understand why "recess is important for kids"?
choices: It lets teachers have more time to plan lessons.
It tells us that kids will like school more if they can focus.
It shows that playing makes kids too tired to pay attention.
It means kids can do their schoolwork better when they return to class.
questions.correct: It means kids can do their schoolwork better when they return to class.
questions.hint: Look at the part of the passage that talks about kids focusing better in class to see why that makes recess important.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The author of the article wanted kids to drink more water every day. She explained that water helps your body in many important ways. Most of all, she said, drinking water helps your brain work better so you can think clearly and learn new things. Which of these is a reason the author gives for why you should drink more water?
choices: Water helps you grow very tall.
Water helps your brain work better.
questions.correct: Water helps your brain work better.
questions.hint: Look at the sentences that tell you *why* drinking water is good for your body.

questions.prompt:
Helmets should be worn when riding a bike. They protect your head if you fall. Studies show helmets reduce head injuries by 85%.

What supports the author's point that helmets are important?
questions.hint: The author uses a fact with a number to support the point. Which sentence has the number?

questions.prompt: Read the short text. "You should always wear a helmet when you ride your bike. Wearing a helmet is important because it keeps your head safe if you fall down. So, remember your helmet!" Which statement best shares the author's main point and the reason that supports it?
choices: The author's point is that bikes are fun, and the reason is they go fast.
The author's point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall.
The author's point is that helmets are colorful, and the reason is they are made of plastic.
The author's point is you might fall down, and the reason is you need to practice riding.
questions.correct: The author's point is to wear a helmet, and the reason is it keeps your head safe if you fall.
questions.hint: Reread the second sentence in the text to find what the author says is important and why.

questions.prompt:
Schools should have gardens. Gardens teach kids about science. They also help kids eat healthier because kids are more likely to eat vegetables they grew themselves.

Which reason explains HOW gardens help kids eat healthier?
choices: Kids eat vegetables they grew themselves
questions.correct: Kids eat vegetables they grew themselves
questions.hint: Look for the sentence with 'because' — that gives the detailed reason.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Bats are helpful animals. One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests on their crops. Some people are scared of bats. But bats almost never bother people, and they would rather fly away than come close." The author wants you to believe that bats are HELPFUL. Which sentence gives a reason for THAT point?
choices: One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
Bats would rather fly away than come close.
Some people are scared of bats.
questions.correct: One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
questions.hint: The passage makes two different points. Find the reason that matches the point about bats being helpful, not the point about bats being safe.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Kids should walk to school when they can. Walking gives your body exercise before the day even starts. It also means one less car on the road, so the air stays cleaner." Why does having one less car help keep the air cleaner?
choices: Because kids like to walk with their friends.
Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
Because cars are faster than walking.
Because walking gives your body exercise.
questions.correct: Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
questions.hint: The author never spells out WHY fewer cars help. Use the clue "one less car... so the air stays cleaner" to figure out what cars must be doing to the air.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Libraries are great places for kids. At the library, you can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want. Libraries are usually very quiet. Some libraries were built a long, long time ago." Which sentence best supports the point that libraries are GREAT for kids?
choices: Some libraries were built a long, long time ago.
Libraries are great places for kids.
You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
questions.correct: You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
questions.hint: Every choice is true, but only one gives a REASON that shows why libraries are great for kids. Watch out for facts that are just interesting and for a choice that only repeats the point.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Read the label before you buy a snack. The label tells you how much sugar is inside. It also shows if there is something that might make you sick, like peanuts." Why does the author mention that the label shows peanuts?
choices: To tell you that all snacks are bad for you.
To show that peanuts taste good.
To show that labels are hard to read.
To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
questions.correct: To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
questions.hint: Ask what job the peanut detail is doing. How does knowing about peanuts help with the author's big point about reading the label?

questions.prompt: Read this: "Dogs make good pets for families. A dog will play with children for hours. A dog can also learn to sit and stay when you teach it." The author wants to add ONE more reason that dogs make good pets. Which sentence should the author add?
choices: A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
A dog will bark loudly all through the night.
Dogs need to visit the vet once every year.
Dogs come in many different sizes and colors.
questions.correct: A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
questions.hint: A new reason has to make the author's point stronger, not weaker. Which sentence gives a real reason dogs are GOOD pets, instead of a plain fact or a downside?

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the most important points presented by two texts on the same topic.
standards.parent_tip: Read two short articles about the same topic and help your child find what each one focuses on differently.

questions.prompt: Text A says dogs are great pets because they are loyal. Text B says dogs are great pets because they can be trained. What do BOTH texts agree on?
questions.hint: What main point do both texts share?

questions.prompt: Read Text A and Text B about the ocean.Text A: The ocean is a big home for many amazing animals! Whales are the biggest animals in the ocean. They swim and sing loud songs. Small, colorful fish dart through coral reefs. Crabs walk sideways on the sandy bottom. Jellyfish float and glow. All these animals need a clean ocean to live and play.Text B: Our ocean needs help to stay clean! Sometimes, trash like plastic bottles and bags floats into the ocean. This trash can hurt ocean animals. Fish might try to eat plastic, or turtles can get tangled. We can help by putting our trash in a bin and recycling. Keeping the ocean clean helps all the animals stay safe and healthy.After reading both texts, what is the biggest difference between what Text A and Text B teach us?
choices: Both texts teach us about how big the ocean is.
Text A is about animals that live on land, and Text B is about ocean animals.
Text A describes different kinds of ocean animals, and Text B explains how to protect the ocean from trash.
Both texts tell us how to swim with whales.
questions.correct: Text A describes different kinds of ocean animals, and Text B explains how to protect the ocean from trash.
questions.hint: Think about the main topic for Text A (what it mostly talks about) and the main topic for Text B (what it mostly talks about). How are those main topics different?

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Bats use sound to find food in the dark." Text B: "Bats eat insects and help farmers by keeping pests away."

What does Text B tell you that Text A does not?
questions.hint: Read Text B — what information is new and not in Text A?

questions.prompt: Read these two texts. Text 1: 'Dogs are playful pets. They have soft fur. Dogs love to run and fetch balls. They often make a barking sound. Dogs need to go outside for walks.' Text 2: 'Cats are quiet pets. They also have soft fur. Cats like to nap and play with string. They often make a meowing sound. Cats like to stay indoors.' Which statement is true about BOTH texts?
choices: Both pets need to go outside for walks.
Both pets make a barking sound.
Both pets like to play with string.
questions.hint: Look closely at what Text 1 and Text 2 say about how the animals feel.

questions.prompt:
Text A says: "Exercise keeps your heart healthy." Text B says: "Exercise helps you sleep better and makes your bones strong."

Which benefit is mentioned in Text A but NOT Text B?
questions.hint: Read Text A carefully — which benefit does it mention that Text B doesn't?

questions.prompt:
Two short texts about bees.

Text A: Bees help flowers grow. When a bee lands on a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries that dust to the next flower. This helps the flower make seeds.

Text B: Bees are busy workers. A bee flies from flower to flower all day. It carries sweet food back to its hive to feed the baby bees.

Both texts tell you that a bee ____.
choices: moves from one flower to another
carries yellow dust on its legs
feeds the baby bees in the hive
questions.correct: moves from one flower to another
questions.hint: Cross out any idea that only ONE text mentions. Keep the idea both texts say in their own words.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about rain.

Text A: Rain is good for us. It fills the rivers we drink from and helps the crops on farms grow tall.

Text B: Too much rain can be a problem. When rain falls hard for many days, rivers can flood and cover the roads.

How are the two texts DIFFERENT?
choices: Text A says rain floods roads, but Text B says rain fills rivers
Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
Both texts say rain is dangerous
Text A is about rivers, and Text B is about the sun
questions.correct: Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
questions.hint: Ask what MAIN point each text is making about rain, then find the choice that matches each one correctly.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about penguins.

Text A: Penguins have wings, but they cannot fly. They use their wings like paddles to swim fast and catch fish in the cold water.

Text B: Penguins stay warm in the freezing cold. Their thick feathers and a layer of fat keep the icy water away from their skin.

Both texts help you understand that penguins ____.
choices: have thick feathers to stay warm
paddle with their wings to swim
live where it is cold and near water
cannot use their wings to fly
questions.correct: live where it is cold and near water
questions.hint: Look for the idea you can find clues for in BOTH texts, even if neither says it in those exact words.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about a class garden.

Text A: Our class grew tomatoes. We watered the plants every morning, and after many weeks the red tomatoes were ready to pick.

Text B: Our class grew sunflowers. We gave them water each day, and the tall yellow flowers took the whole spring to bloom.

What is ONE way both classes' plants were the same?
choices: Both plants grew tall in one week
Both plants needed water often to grow
questions.correct: Both plants needed water often to grow
questions.hint: Find something that is true in Text A AND true in Text B. Two of the choices are only true for one text.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about lunch at school.

Text A: Maya thinks packing lunch is best. You get to choose your own food, and you never have to wait in a long line.

Text B: Sam thinks buying lunch is best. The food is warm, and you do not have to carry a heavy bag to school.

What do Maya and Sam DISAGREE about?
choices: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
Whether school food is warm enough
Whether lunch lines are too long
Whether lunch bags are too heavy
questions.correct: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
questions.hint: A disagreement is the BIG question both writers answer differently, not just one small reason.

standards.standard_description: By end of year, read and comprehend informational texts in the grades 2-3 text complexity band proficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to read nonfiction about topics they enjoy to build reading stamina.

questions.prompt: Based on the text, why would an octopus change its color?
choices: To show how happy it is.
questions.hint: Reread the sentence that explains what octopuses do when they change color.

questions.prompt:
Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to power a TV for three hours. Americans throw away enough cans each year to build more than 20 Eiffel Towers.

What is the author trying to show with these facts?
choices: The Eiffel Tower is made of cans
Recycling cans saves a lot of energy
questions.correct: Recycling cans saves a lot of energy
questions.hint: The author compares cans to TV power and Eiffel Towers. Why? To show recycling matters!

questions.prompt:
Polar bears have black skin under their white fur. The white fur helps them hide in the snow. Their large paws work like snowshoes, helping them walk on ice.

What helps polar bears walk on ice?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — what works like snowshoes?

questions.prompt: Over many years, what causes a volcano to slowly get taller and wider?
choices: The ground under the volcano stretches and pushes it up.
New layers of hardened lava build up on its sides.
Heavy rains wash dirt and rocks to pile around it.
Wind blows sand and dust onto the volcano's top.
questions.correct: New layers of hardened lava build up on its sides.
questions.hint: Look for the part of the passage that explains how volcanoes grow over time.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Beavers cut down trees with their strong front teeth. They drag the branches into a river and stack them until the water stops flowing. This makes a deep, calm pond behind the pile of wood. Beavers build their homes in the middle of these ponds. The deep water keeps hungry wolves and bears from reaching them.

Why do beavers stack branches until the water stops flowing?
choices: To make deep water that keeps them safe
To help wolves and bears cross the river
To have extra branches to eat later
To clear the trees away from the river
questions.correct: To make deep water that keeps them safe
questions.hint: Follow the chain: stopping the water makes a pond, and the last sentence tells you what the deep water does.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Some frogs freeze almost solid in winter. Their hearts stop beating and they do not breathe. To an animal that finds one, the frog seems dead. But sugar in the frog's body keeps the inside of its cells from turning hard. When spring warms the ground, the frog thaws out and hops away.

What is the sugar's most important job for the frog?
choices: It warms the frog up when spring comes
It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
It keeps the frog's heart beating all winter
It makes the frog taste bad to other animals
questions.correct: It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
questions.hint: Find the sentence that says what the sugar does, and notice the word 'But' before it.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Long ago, sailors could not tell how far east or west they were. Many ships got lost and crashed on rocks. Then a clockmaker built a clock that kept perfect time even on a rocking ship. By comparing the clock's time to the sun, sailors finally knew their place on the sea. After that, far fewer ships were lost.

What happened because the clockmaker built his special clock?
choices: Sailors no longer needed to watch the sun
The sea became calmer for the ships
Fewer ships got lost and crashed
Ships could sail faster than before
questions.correct: Fewer ships got lost and crashed
questions.hint: Read to the very end. What changed for the ships after the clock was invented?

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

A cactus lives where rain almost never falls. Instead of flat leaves, it has sharp spines, so very little water leaks out into the dry air. Its thick stem swells up like a sponge to store water from one rare rain. Roots spread out just under the sand to soak up a shower fast, before the sun dries the ground.

Why does having spines instead of flat leaves help the cactus?
choices: It can grow taller in the sand
It can store more water in its roots
It can soak up rain more quickly
It loses less water into the dry air
questions.correct: It loses less water into the dry air
questions.hint: The sentence about spines has a clue word 'so' that tells you what the spines do.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Honey bees dance to talk. When a bee finds flowers full of food, it flies home and dances in a circle. The other bees crowd close and feel the dance with their feelers. Then they fly straight out to find the same flowers. A bee that finds no food does not dance at all.

What can you tell about a hive where the bees are NOT dancing?
choices: The bees have not found flowers with food
The bees are dancing in a different way
The bees are too tired to fly
The bees have too much food to eat
questions.correct: The bees have not found flowers with food
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you when a bee will not dance. Use that rule backwards.

standards.standard_description: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice sounding out new words together — break them into smaller parts.

questions.prompt: Which word has a long vowel sound?
questions.hint: A long vowel says its own name. Which word has the 'ay' sound?

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **playing**?
questions.hint: Remove the ending '-ing' — what word is left?

questions.prompt: If you are *not feeling happy*, which word best describes how you feel?
questions.hint: Remember that the letters 'un-' at the start of a word often mean 'not' or 'the opposite of'.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **pumpkin**?
questions.hint: Clap it out: pump-kin. How many claps?

questions.prompt: What sound does the 'au' make in the word 'because'?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to how you say the middle part of 'because'. Think about other words where 'au' or 'aw' make a similar sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has a long i sound? **stick, strike, string, script**
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. A silent e at the end often makes the vowel say its own name.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **bread**? **bead, meant, dream, bean**
questions.hint: The letters 'ea' can say two sounds. Listen for the short e sound you hear in bread.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as **first**? **farm, nurse, form, fair**
questions.hint: Different letters can make the same r-controlled sound. Listen to the middle of 'first' and match it.

questions.prompt: Which word starts with an open syllable that has a long vowel, like **tiger** (ti-ger)? **rabbit, robot, napkin, basket**
questions.hint: Clap the word into two parts. If the first part ends in a vowel, that vowel is usually long.

questions.prompt: What is the base (root) word in **unhappily**?
questions.hint: Peel off the beginning part and the ending part. What word is left in the middle?

standards.standard_description: Distinguish long and short vowels when reading regularly spelled one-syllable words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice identifying short vowels (like 'a' in 'cat') vs long vowels (like 'a' in 'cake').

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **a** sound?
questions.hint: Short 'a' sounds like the 'a' in 'cat.' Which word has that sound?

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **o** sound?
questions.hint: Long 'o' says its own name, like 'oh.' Which word sounds like that?

questions.prompt: The word **bed** has a —
questions.hint: Say 'bed' out loud. Does the 'e' say its name or make a short sound?

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **e** sound? Bread, Sweep, Deck, Kept
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does the vowel say its own name (like the /ee/ in 'see'), or is it a quick short sound? Watch out — the letters 'ea' don't always make a long sound.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **i** sound? Child, Print, Find, Might
questions.hint: Try each word: does the 'i' say its name (/eye/) or the quick /ih/ sound? Some words hide a long i even without a silent e.

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **a** sound? Draft, Sprain, Blast, Grand
questions.hint: Ignore the beginning blend and listen only to the middle vowel. Does 'a' say its name /ay/, or the short /a/ as in 'cat'?

questions.prompt: Which word has a LONG **u** sound? Trust, Prune, Brush, Crunch
questions.hint: Listen to the middle vowel after the blend. Does 'u' say its name (/oo/ as in 'tune') or the short /uh/ sound? A silent e at the end can change it.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **o** sound? Coach, Frost, Float, Groan
questions.hint: Say each word and listen to the 'o'. Does it say its name /oh/, or the quick short /o/ as in 'dog'? The 'oa' team is a clue for one sound.

standards.standard_description: Know spelling-sound correspondences for additional common vowel teams.
standards.parent_tip: Practice vowel teams like 'oa' in boat, 'ee' in tree, and 'ai' in rain.

questions.prompt: In the word **boat,** what sound does the vowel team **oa** make?
questions.hint: Say 'boat' — the 'oa' makes the 'oh' sound!

questions.prompt: Which word has the **ee** vowel team?
questions.hint: Look for two e's side by side!

questions.prompt: Which word correctly completes the sentence and has the same vowel sound as **rain**? We went to the park to ______.
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the long 'a' sound in 'rain' and find the word that makes that same sound and fits the sentence.

questions.prompt: What sound does the 'oa' vowel team make in the word 'goat'?
questions.hint: Think about how you say the word 'goat' and focus on the sound the letters 'oa' make together.

questions.prompt: Which vowel team is in the word **loud**?
questions.hint: Look at the middle of 'loud' — which two vowels are together?

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ea** usually says long e, like in **beach**. But it can also say short e, like in **head**. Which word below has **ea** saying the SHORT e sound?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud. Listen for the one where 'ea' sounds like the 'e' in 'bed,' not the 'e' in 'see.'

questions.prompt: The vowel team **oo** makes two sounds. In **moon** it is long, but in **book** it is short. Which word has the SAME **oo** sound as **book**?
questions.hint: Say 'book,' then say each choice. Find the one whose 'oo' sounds just like it, not like 'zoo.'

questions.prompt: The vowel team **ie** can say long i, like in **pie**, or long e, like in **chief**. Which word has **ie** saying the long e sound?
questions.hint: Read each word. Which one rhymes with a long-e word like 'shield,' not with 'my'?

questions.prompt: The word **rain** makes its long a sound with the vowel team **ai**. Which word makes the long a sound with a DIFFERENT vowel team?
questions.hint: Three of these spell long a the same way as 'rain.' Look for the one that uses a-y instead of a-i.

questions.prompt: A vowel team is two vowels working together to make one sound, like **oa** or **ee**. Which word below has NO vowel team?
questions.hint: Look at the vowels in each word. Which word has only one single vowel with no partner next to it?

standards.standard_description: Decode regularly spelled two-syllable words with long vowels.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child break two-syllable words into parts and sound out each part.

questions.prompt: Break the word **cupcake** into two syllables:
questions.hint: This is a compound word made of two smaller words!

questions.prompt: Which two-syllable word has a long **i** sound?
questions.hint: Say each word. Which one has the 'eye' sound in it?

questions.prompt: Which choice shows how to correctly say the word **robot**?
questions.hint: Remember how to sound out two-syllable words that have a long vowel sound.

questions.prompt: Break **open** into syllables:
questions.hint: The 'o' is long and open — it gets its own syllable!

questions.prompt:
Three of these two-syllable words have a long vowel sound. Which word does NOT?

music   human   tulip   muffin
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and split it in two. If the first syllable ends in a vowel (mu-sic), that vowel is usually long. Which one has a closed first syllable with a short sound?

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **o** sound?

robot   rocket   pocket   locket
questions.hint: Break each word after the first vowel or after the first consonant. If the first syllable ends in the vowel, the o stays long. Try ro-bot versus rock-et.

questions.prompt: Which shows the correct way to break **tiger** into two syllables so the **i** stays long?
questions.hint: When one consonant sits between two vowels, try splitting BEFORE the consonant first. That leaves the first vowel at the end of its syllable, so it says its long name.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **a** sound?

mistake   basket   napkin   rabbit
questions.hint: Read the second syllable of each word. Look for a silent e on the end that can jump back and make the a say its name.

questions.prompt:
Which two-syllable word has a long **e** sound?

zebra   lemon   wagon   rabbit
questions.hint: Split each word into two parts. If the first syllable ends in the vowel (ze-bra), that vowel is long. Compare ze-bra with lem-on.

standards.standard_description: Decode words with common prefixes and suffixes.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child that 'un-' means 'not' and '-ful' means 'full of' to help decode new words.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-ful** in **hopeful** means —
questions.hint: Hope + ful = full of hope!

questions.prompt: What does it mean if something is "helpful"?
questions.hint: The word "helpful" has the suffix "-ful." Think about what "-ful" means when you add it to a word like "help."

questions.prompt: Read these four words: uncle, under, until, unpack. In which word is un- a PREFIX that means "not" or "reverse"?
questions.hint: Cover the un- and see if a real base word is left. "pack" is a word, but "cle," "der," and "til" are not.

questions.prompt: The word grinned was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: When -ed was added, the last consonant was doubled. Take off -ed and one extra n to find the short base word.

questions.prompt: The word riding was made by adding -ing to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: An e was dropped before adding -ing. Add the e back to the base, not just the letters you see.

questions.prompt: The word cried was made by adding -ed to a base word. Which is the base word?
questions.hint: A y was changed to i before -ed was added. Change the i back to y to find the base word.

questions.prompt: Read these four words: red, reptile, ready, rewrite. In which word is re- a PREFIX that means "again"?
questions.hint: Cover the re- and check for a real base word that could happen "again." "write" works; "d," "ptile," and "ady" do not.

standards.standard_description: Identify words with inconsistent but common spelling-sound correspondences.
standards.parent_tip: Some words don't follow the rules — help your child learn tricky words like 'said' and 'have' by sight.

questions.prompt: If you were trying to sound out each word below, which one would be the trickiest to read because its letter sounds don't follow the usual phonics rules?
questions.hint: Look for the word where the letters don't make the sounds you expect them to make when you try to read it aloud.

questions.prompt: The word **have** is tricky because —
choices: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short
The 'a' doesn't make the expected sound
questions.correct: It ends with 'e' but the 'a' is short
questions.hint: Usually a word ending in 'e' has a long vowel. But 'have' has a short 'a'!

questions.prompt: Which word has a silent letter?
questions.hint: Say each word. In one of them, a letter is there but you don't hear it.

questions.prompt: In the word **love,** the **o** makes the sound of —
questions.hint: Say 'love' and 'cup.' The vowel sounds the same!

questions.prompt: Which word below has a part that sounds different from how you would usually expect those letters to sound?
questions.hint: Read each word aloud and listen carefully. Which one has a letter or letters that don't make the sound you usually expect them to make?

questions.prompt: In three of these words the letters **ea** make the long-e sound you hear in *eat*. Which word is the tricky one, where **ea** makes a SHORT-e sound instead?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the middle sound. Three match 'eat' — one sounds like 'bed'.

questions.prompt: The word **said** does not sound the way its letters look — it sounds like /sed/. Which word below **rhymes with said**?
questions.hint: Ignore how 'said' is spelled and listen to how it sounds when you talk. Which word ends with that same sound?

questions.prompt: In the word **knot**, the first letter is silent — you only hear /not/. Which other word begins with a SILENT letter?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three start with the /n/ sound you can hear right away — one hides a silent letter before the 'n'.

questions.prompt: Words that end in silent **e** usually make the vowel say its long name, like *bone* and *home*. Which word BREAKS that rule — it ends in **e** but the vowel is not long?
questions.hint: Say each word. Three make the 'o' say its name (long o). One sounds like 'kum' — that's the tricky one.

questions.prompt: The word **would** is tricky: the **l** is silent and it sounds like /wood/. Which word below **rhymes with would**?
questions.hint: Say 'would' out loud, then find the word that ends with the same sound — watch out for words that just look similar.

standards.standard_description: Recognize and read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice sight words like 'people,' 'again,' and 'friend' — these need to be memorized, not sounded out.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct spelling:
questions.hint: Remember: 'i' before 'e' in friend!

questions.prompt: Choose the correct spelling of the word that means **one more time**:
questions.hint: This tricky word ends with '-ain' like 'rain.'

questions.prompt: Which word is an irregularly spelled word you need to memorize?
questions.hint: Three of these words can be sounded out. One is tricky and needs to be memorized.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "for the reason that," as in "I stayed inside ___ it was raining." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word breaks the rules. Picture the whole word the way you have seen it in books, and remember it ends with the little word 'cause.'

questions.prompt: Three of these words follow the regular sounding-out rules. One is an irregular word you must know by heart because the letters do not make their usual sounds. Which one is the irregular word?
questions.hint: Try to sound out each word. The one where the 'ai' does NOT say its normal sound is the tricky one.

questions.prompt: In one of these words the letters 'gh' make the /f/ sound (like in 'ph'). Which word is it?
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen for an /f/ sound at the end. In the others the 'gh' is silent.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "as much as you need," as in "I have ___ money." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This is an irregular word. It ends with 'gh' even though you hear an /f/ sound. Picture how it looks on the page.

questions.prompt: One of these words is spelled correctly. It means "was able to," as in "I ___ hear the bird." Which spelling is correct?
questions.hint: This word has a letter you do not hear. Remember it belongs to the 'could, would, should' family that all share the same tricky ending.

standards.standard_description: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.
standards.parent_tip: Listen to your child read aloud — encourage them to read smoothly and with expression.

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a fun story to your classmates. Which way of reading would help everyone understand the story best?
choices: Reading very fast, almost like a race.
Reading each word slowly, stopping often to figure it out.
Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.
Reading so quietly that only you can hear yourself.
questions.correct: Reading smoothly, like you are telling the story, and saying most words correctly.
questions.hint: Think about how good readers make a story easy for others to listen to and understand.

questions.prompt: When you see an exclamation mark (!) at the end of a sentence, you should read it with —
questions.hint: An exclamation mark means the sentence has strong emotion!

questions.prompt: If you don't understand a sentence after reading it, what should you do?
choices: Ask someone to read the whole book
questions.hint: Good readers go back and reread to make sure they understand.

questions.prompt:
"Stop!" yelled the crossing guard.

How should you read the word **Stop**?
questions.hint: The crossing guard 'yelled' — so you should read it like a yell!

questions.prompt:
Which of these words has TWO syllables? Read each one carefully:

through    yellow    bright    scratched
questions.hint: Clap the word out loud. Count the beats where your chin drops, not the letters you see.

questions.prompt:
Which word RHYMES with the word 'said'? (Say 'said' out loud first: it sounds like /sed/.)

seed    red    sad    side
questions.hint: Listen to the ending sound, not the spelling. Say 'said' slowly and match the last part.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same long-i sound and silent-e pattern as 'stripe'?

slide    sniff    spill    sprint
questions.hint: Find the silent e on the end that makes the i say its name. Cover the blend and check the vowel.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as 'boil'? (Say 'boil': it has the /oy/ sound in the middle.)

toy    book    boat    bowl
questions.hint: The /oy/ sound can be spelled 'oi' or 'oy'. Listen for the sound, not the letters.

questions.prompt:
Which word BEGINS with the same sound as 'gentle'? (Say 'gentle': it starts with a /j/ sound.)

giant    goat    great    gum
questions.hint: The letter g can say /g/ or /j/. Say each word and listen to the very first sound.

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text with purpose and understanding.
standards.parent_tip: Before reading, set a purpose: 'Let's read to find out how butterflies are born.'

questions.prompt: What did the farmer do to help his garden grow before summer?
choices: He watered the seeds and planted them.
He made soup with his vegetables.
He waited for the snow to fall.
questions.correct: He watered the seeds and planted them.
questions.hint: Look at the first two sentences to find out what the farmer did to start his garden and keep it healthy.

questions.prompt: Before you start reading, it helps to ask yourself —
choices: What do I want to learn from this?
questions.correct: What do I want to learn from this?
questions.hint: Setting a purpose helps you focus while reading.

questions.prompt:
Dolphins are mammals, not fish. They breathe air and feed milk to their babies. Dolphins are very smart and can learn tricks.

What makes dolphins different from fish?
choices: They breathe air and feed milk to babies
questions.correct: They breathe air and feed milk to babies
questions.hint: The passage says dolphins are mammals, not fish. What makes them mammals?

questions.prompt:
Ben's favorite book is about space. He reads it every week because he wants to be an astronaut someday.

Why does Ben read the space book so often?
choices: He wants to be an astronaut
It's the only book he has
questions.correct: He wants to be an astronaut
questions.hint: The sentence after 'because' tells you the reason.

questions.prompt:
Earthworms help gardens by making tunnels in the soil. The tunnels let air and water reach plant roots.

How do earthworm tunnels help plants?
choices: They make the garden look nice
They let air and water reach the roots
questions.correct: They let air and water reach the roots
questions.hint: Read the second sentence — what do the tunnels let through?

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with the word great?

great
questions.hint: Say each word out loud and listen to the ending sound. Don't trust the spelling — 'ea' can trick you.

questions.prompt:
In which word does the letter c make the /s/ sound (like in 'ice')?

cent   cave   cup   cot
questions.hint: Look at the letter right after the c. C is soft (/s/) before e, i, or y, and hard (/k/) before a, o, or u.

questions.prompt:
How many syllables are in the word important?

important
questions.hint: Clap once for each vowel sound you hear: im - por - tant. Count the beats, not the letters.

questions.prompt:
Which word has a silent letter (a letter you do NOT hear when you say it)?

knee   nest   note   need
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does every letter make a sound? Watch the very first letter of each word.

questions.prompt:
Which word has the same vowel sound as the word boy?

boy
questions.hint: Listen for the /oy/ sound in the middle. Remember that 'oi' and 'oy' can spell the very same sound.

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression.
standards.parent_tip: Model reading aloud with expression — change your voice for different characters and show excitement!

questions.prompt: How should you read this sentence? "I can't believe we won the game!"
questions.hint: The exclamation mark and 'I can't believe' show strong, happy feelings!

questions.prompt: When you read a question aloud, your voice should —
questions.hint: Try saying 'Are you coming?' — notice how your voice rises at the end?

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a story out loud to your younger brother or sister. What is the best way to read so they can easily understand and enjoy the story?
choices: Read the words very quickly so you can finish the book fast.
Read each word super slowly, one by one, with long pauses.
Read quietly to yourself so no one else can hear.
Read at a smooth speed, not too fast and not too slow, making the story sound clear.
questions.correct: Read at a smooth speed, not too fast and not too slow, making the story sound clear.
questions.hint: Think about how fast you should read so that the person listening can understand every word and follow the story easily.

questions.prompt: How should you read this line from a character who is scared? "W-what was that noise?"
questions.hint: The dashes in 'W-what' show stuttering — the character is scared!

questions.prompt: Someone asks you: "WHO ate the last apple?" You answer by reading aloud: "Sam ate the last apple." Which word should you stress (say the strongest) to answer the question?
questions.hint: Stress the word that gives the exact information the question is asking for.

questions.prompt: Read this line the right way: "We won!" she whispered. How should you read the part "We won!"?
choices: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.correct: Softly, in a whisper, but excited
questions.hint: The words right after the quote (the tag) tell you HOW the person is speaking, even when there is an exclamation mark.

questions.prompt: Where should you take a short pause when you read this sentence aloud? "When the bell rang, the class lined up."
questions.hint: A comma is a signal to take a quick breath or pause.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence aloud: "You ate the whole thing?" It has no question word like "what" or "who," but it ends with a question mark. How should your voice sound at the end?
questions.hint: A question mark tells your voice to rise at the end, even when the sentence looks like a statement.

questions.prompt: You are reading a story aloud. It says: "The house was silent. Slowly, quietly, she opened the creaky door." How should you read this part?
choices: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.correct: Slowly and quietly, to build suspense
questions.hint: Let the mood words — silent, slowly, quietly — guide how fast and how loud you read.

standards.standard_description: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding, rereading as necessary.
standards.parent_tip: When your child reads a word wrong and the sentence doesn't make sense, encourage them to try again.

questions.prompt: You read: "The dog buried a bone in the yard." But you accidentally read **bone** as "bun." What should you do?
choices: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense
Ask someone to read for you
questions.correct: Go back and reread because 'bun' doesn't make sense
questions.hint: Does 'The dog buried a bun in the yard' make sense? If not, try again!

questions.prompt: What is a good strategy when a sentence doesn't make sense?
choices: Reread the sentence and try the hard word again
questions.correct: Reread the sentence and try the hard word again
questions.hint: Good readers slow down and try again when something doesn't sound right.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "Yesterday I read a whole book about frogs." How should you say the word **read** here?
choices: Like "red," because the sentence says it already happened (yesterday)
Like "reed," because you are saying it right now
questions.correct: Like "red," because the sentence says it already happened (yesterday)
questions.hint: The word 'read' can sound two ways. Look for a clue in the sentence that tells you WHEN it happened.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The farmer will lead the cow to the barn." Which is the right way to say **lead**?
choices: It rhymes with "feed," because he is guiding the cow
It rhymes with "bed," because it is the heavy gray metal
questions.correct: It rhymes with "feed," because he is guiding the cow
questions.hint: 'Lead' can name a metal OR mean to guide someone. Reread the sentence to see which meaning fits the cow.

questions.prompt: A reader read this sentence: "Mom put the groceries in the trunk of the car." She first said the word **trunk** as "truck." What is the best fix?
choices: Skip the word and read on
Reread it as 'trunk'—groceries go in a car's trunk, and the word ends in -nk
Keep 'truck' because it looks almost the same
questions.correct: Reread it as 'trunk'—groceries go in a car's trunk, and the word ends in -nk
questions.hint: 'Truck' and 'trunk' look alike. Check the LAST sounds of the word and whether it makes sense in the car.

questions.prompt: A reader read this sentence: "The library is a quiet place to read." He said the word **quiet** as "quite." What should he do?
choices: Keep 'quite' because the letters look the same
Reread it—'quiet' has two beats (qui-et), means no noise, and fits a library
questions.correct: Reread it—'quiet' has two beats (qui-et), means no noise, and fits a library
questions.hint: 'Quiet' and 'quite' use the same letters in a different order. Clap the syllables and think about what a library is like.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The brave knight rode his horse to the castle." A reader said the word **knight** as "night." Which fix is best?
choices: Keep 'knight'—it starts with a silent k and means a man in armor, which fits riding a horse
Change it to 'night,' the dark time of day
questions.correct: Keep 'knight'—it starts with a silent k and means a man in armor, which fits riding a horse
questions.hint: 'Knight' and 'night' sound exactly the same. Use the spelling (silent k) AND the meaning of the sentence to decide.

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases.
standards.parent_tip: When your child encounters an unknown word, help them use the sentence around it as a clue.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The bag was so light that I could lift it with one finger.' What does the word light mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: Think about how the bag feels when you pick it up, not about seeing.

questions.prompt: The prefix un- means 'not.' What does the word unhappy mean?
questions.hint: Add the meaning of the prefix un- to the word happy.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The soup was so scalding that Mia had to wait for it to cool before eating.' What does scalding most likely mean?
questions.hint: Look at the clue 'wait for it to cool' to figure out how the soup felt.

questions.prompt: A compound word is made of two smaller words put together. What does the compound word sailboat most likely mean?
choices: a boat that moves using a sail
questions.correct: a boat that moves using a sail
questions.hint: Break the word into its two smaller words and join their meanings.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The baseball player will bat first in the game.' What does the word bat mean in this sentence?
choices: to take a turn hitting the ball
a stick used in a game
questions.correct: to take a turn hitting the ball
questions.hint: The words 'will bat' tell you it is an action the player does, not a thing.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The elephant used its trunk to spray water over its dusty back.' What does the word trunk mean in THIS sentence?
choices: the long nose of an elephant
the back part of a car
the thick stem of a tree
a big box for storing things
questions.correct: the long nose of an elephant
questions.hint: The word trunk has many meanings. Look at the other words in the sentence to see which meaning fits what an elephant would use to spray water.

questions.prompt: Ben was not just a little scared during the storm. He hid under the covers and shook. Which word best shows that Ben was the MOST scared?
questions.hint: These words all mean scared, but some mean only a tiny bit scared. Which one shows the strongest, biggest fear?

questions.prompt: The suffix -less means 'without.' Read: 'The little green snake was harmless, so it would never bite or hurt anyone.' What does harmless most likely mean?
questions.hint: Take off the suffix -less and remember it means 'without.' Then use the clue that the snake 'would never bite or hurt.'

questions.prompt: The prefix pre- means 'before.' Read: 'Our teacher showed us a preview of the movie before we watched the whole thing.' What does preview mean?
choices: a look at something after it ends
a look at something before it happens
questions.correct: a look at something before it happens
questions.hint: Break the word into its prefix pre- ('before') and the part -view ('a look'). Put the two meanings together.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'Please save me a seat next to you at lunch today.' What does the word save mean in THIS sentence?
choices: to put money away in a bank
questions.hint: Save can mean many things. Ask what makes sense for a seat at lunch, not for money or danger.

standards.standard_description: Use sentence-level context as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child to look at the words AROUND a tricky word for clues about what it means.

questions.prompt:
"The enormous elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo."

The word **enormous** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says it was the BIGGEST animal. What word means very big?

questions.prompt:
"The frigid wind made everyone shiver and bundle up in their coats."

The word **frigid** means —
questions.hint: People shivered and bundled up. What kind of wind would make you do that?

questions.prompt:
"After the long hike, we sat down to rest on the grassy bank and watched the river flow by."

In this sentence, the word **bank** means —
choices: The ground along the edge of a river
A place where people keep their money
questions.correct: The ground along the edge of a river
questions.hint: The word bank can mean more than one thing. Read the whole sentence and find the clue that tells you which meaning fits HERE.

questions.prompt:
"The tiny mouse crept across the kitchen floor, careful not to wake the sleeping cat."

The word **crept** tells you the mouse moved —
questions.hint: Ask WHY the mouse moved that way. What does 'careful not to wake the cat' tell you about how it was moving?

questions.prompt:
"The soup tasted so bland that Grandpa added salt and pepper to give it more flavor."

The word **bland** means —
questions.hint: Why would someone ADD salt and pepper to something? What must the food have been missing?

questions.prompt:
"The elephant reached up with its long trunk and sprayed water all over its back."

In this sentence, the word **trunk** means —
choices: The back part of a car
A big box for storing things
The bottom part of a tree
questions.hint: The word trunk has several meanings. Which one can 'reach up and spray water'? Let the sentence decide.

questions.prompt:
"Instead of just walking, the excited puppy dashed across the yard to greet its owner."

The word **dashed** means the puppy —
choices: Walked in a slow, calm way
questions.hint: The sentence says 'instead of just walking.' That contrast is your clue to how fast the puppy went.

questions.prompt:
"The soup was so hot that Maria had to wait for it to cool down before eating."

The word **hot** means —
questions.hint: She had to wait for the soup to COOL DOWN. Cooling down means going from what temperature?

questions.prompt:
"The puppy was tiny — small enough to fit in one hand."

The word **tiny** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the puppy could fit in one hand. That tells you it is very ___.

questions.prompt:
"Ben felt happy and smiled big when he opened his birthday present."

The word **happy** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says Ben SMILED BIG. What kind of feeling makes you smile?

questions.prompt:
"The road was so wet after the rain that the cars splashed through puddles."

The word **wet** means —
questions.hint: It rained and there were PUDDLES. Puddles are made of what?

questions.prompt:
"The box was heavy, and Sam grunted as he lifted it off the floor."

The word **heavy** means —
questions.hint: Sam GRUNTED when he lifted the box. That shows the box was ___ to lift.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of the new word formed when a known prefix is added to a known word.
standards.parent_tip: Practice prefix meanings: 'un-' = not, 're-' = again, 'pre-' = before.

questions.prompt: The word **preview** means —
questions.hint: 'Pre-' means 'before.' Pre + view = view before!

questions.prompt: Maria drew her picture, erased the whole thing, and then drew it a second time from the start. Which word tells exactly what Maria did? She ____ her picture.
questions.hint: Break each word into its prefix + 'drew.' Ask what each prefix means: again, before, undo, or wrongly? Match the one that means 'did it again.'

questions.prompt: The workers put the box on the wrong shelf because it was **mislabeled**. In this sentence, mislabeled means the box was labeled —
questions.hint: Cover the word and think about the prefix mis-. Then use the clue 'wrong shelf' to check which meaning of mis- fits.

questions.prompt: The rope was knotted tightly, so the sailor had to **unfasten** it fast before the boat drifted. Unfasten means to —
questions.hint: The prefix un- can reverse an action. Ask: is the sailor tying it up, or taking the tie apart? Let the story tell you.

questions.prompt: Dad left the soup on the stove far too long, and it turned into mush. Which word best describes what Dad did to the soup? He ____ it.
questions.hint: Each choice is a real prefix + 'cooked.' Decide whether 'too long' means too much, too little, again, or beforehand — then pick the matching prefix.

questions.prompt: The bridge was **underbuilt** for such heavy trucks, so it began to crack the first day. Underbuilt means the bridge was built —
questions.hint: Think what under- means when added to a word (like undercooked or underfed). Then use 'began to crack' to confirm which meaning fits.

standards.standard_description: Use a known root word as a clue to the meaning of an unknown word with the same root.
standards.parent_tip: Show your child how knowing 'play' helps them understand 'player,' 'playful,' and 'replay.'

questions.prompt: If **teach** means to help someone learn, what does a **teacher** do?
questions.hint: The root word is 'teach.' A teacher is a person who teaches!

questions.prompt: If someone is *helpless*, what does that mean about them?
choices: They cannot do things by themselves and need help.
questions.correct: They cannot do things by themselves and need help.
questions.hint: Think about what the word "help" means on its own.

questions.prompt: You know the word **agree**. Read this sentence: "The two friends had a **disagreement** about which game to play." What does *disagreement* mean?
choices: A time when they did NOT agree about something
A promise to always agree from now on
A moment when they finally agreed on the game
A fun game that they both wanted to play
questions.correct: A time when they did NOT agree about something
questions.hint: Start with the root you know (agree), then notice the little part 'dis-' stuck on the front. What does 'dis-' usually do to a word's meaning?

questions.prompt: You know the word **wash**. Read this sentence: "Mom said the new plates are **washable**." What does *washable* mean?
choices: The plates are only used on special days
The plates can be washed and used again
The plates are already washed and clean
The plates cannot get wet or they will break
questions.correct: The plates can be washed and used again
questions.hint: You know 'wash.' The ending '-able' tells you something is possible to do. Put the two together.

questions.prompt: You know that **hope** means to wish for something good. Read: "Even after losing, the coach stayed **hopeful** the team would win next time." What does *hopeful* mean?
choices: So sad that there is no hope left
Angry at the team for losing
Full of hope that something good will happen
Sure that the team already won the game
questions.correct: Full of hope that something good will happen
questions.hint: The root 'hope' is inside the word. The ending '-ful' means 'full of.' What is the coach full of?

questions.prompt: You know the word **use**. A grocery bag that is **reusable** is one that you can—
choices: Use only to carry heavy things
Use only one time and then throw away
Never use because it is broken
Use again and again, not just one time
questions.correct: Use again and again, not just one time
questions.hint: There are TWO clues stuck to 'use': 're-' at the front and '-able' at the end. Figure out what each one adds.

questions.prompt: You know the word **fear**, which means to be scared. A knight who runs straight at the dragon and is NOT scared at all is best called—
questions.hint: All four choices come from the root 'fear.' Think about which ending means 'without' — like in 'helpless.'

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge of the meaning of individual words to predict the meaning of compound words.
standards.parent_tip: Play compound word games: 'What two words make up sunflower? sun + flower!'

questions.prompt: What does **sunflower** mean?
choices: A flower that blocks the sun
A flower that faces the sun
questions.correct: A flower that faces the sun
questions.hint: Break it apart: sun + flower. It's a flower connected to the sun!

questions.prompt: If you know what **rain** means and what **coat** means, what does a **raincoat** help you do?
choices: It is a coat you wear to go swimming.
It is a coat made for sunny days.
It is a coat that keeps you dry in the rain.
It is a coat that gets wet in the rain.
questions.correct: It is a coat that keeps you dry in the rain.
questions.hint: Think about what 'rain' is and what a 'coat' is for.

questions.prompt: What is a **bookshelf**?
questions.hint: Book + shelf. Where do you put books?

questions.prompt: If **light** means something that shines and **house** means a building, what is a **lighthouse**?
choices: A building with a bright light to guide ships
questions.correct: A building with a bright light to guide ships
questions.hint: Think about a building (house) with a big shining light. Where would you find this?

questions.prompt: If "rain" means water falling from the sky and a "coat" is clothing you wear, what does a "raincoat" help you do?
choices: Play with your friends in the park.
Keep dry when water falls from the sky.
Stay warm when it is cold outside.
questions.correct: Keep dry when water falls from the sky.
questions.hint: Consider what happens when water falls from the sky and what kind of clothing a coat is.

standards.standard_description: Use glossaries and beginning dictionaries to determine or clarify the meaning of words.
standards.parent_tip: Show your child how to look up words in a dictionary or glossary — practice finding words alphabetically.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: 'Maya was excited to discover a hidden key under the rug.' If you used a dictionary to understand the word 'discover,' which meaning would best fit the sentence?
choices: to clean up a messy area
to find something new or hidden
questions.correct: to find something new or hidden
questions.hint: Imagine you are using a dictionary to look up the word 'discover.' Which definition helps you understand what Maya did with the key?

questions.prompt: A glossary says that 'flutter' means to move wings quickly. If a butterfly starts to flutter, what is it doing?
choices: It is resting on a flower.
It is moving its wings fast.
questions.correct: It is moving its wings fast.
questions.hint: Look back at what the glossary says 'flutter' means.

questions.prompt: A dictionary entry for a word looks like this: penguin (noun): A large bird that lives in cold places, swims very well, and has black and white feathers. It cannot fly.Based on this dictionary entry, which sentence tells you what 'penguin' means?
choices: A small animal that runs fast.
A furry animal that climbs trees.
A big bird that swims and cannot fly.
A fish that lives in warm water.
questions.correct: A big bird that swims and cannot fly.
questions.hint: Look closely at the words that describe what a penguin is in the dictionary entry.

questions.prompt: A dictionary page has the guide word 'ladder' at the top-left corner and 'lamp' at the top-right corner. Guide words tell you the FIRST and LAST word on that page. Which word below would you find on THIS page?
questions.hint: The first two letters 'la' are the same for all of them, so compare the THIRD letter to 'ladder' (d) and 'lamp' (m). Which third letter lands in between?

questions.prompt: You want to look up the word 'hopping' in the dictionary, but dictionaries only list the base word. Which word should you search for to find 'hopping'?
questions.hint: Take off the ending to find the base word. Remember that when a short word adds -ing, it sometimes doubles its last letter first.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: present — 1. (noun) a gift.  2. (verb) to give or show something.  3. (adjective) here; not away.  4. (noun) the time happening right now. Read this sentence: 'Every student was present, so nobody was missing today.' Which meaning of 'present' fits THIS sentence?
questions.hint: Ignore what 'present' means most often (a gift). Use the clue 'nobody was missing' to decide which numbered meaning matches.

questions.prompt: A glossary shows: spring — 1. the season after winter.  2. to jump up quickly.  3. a coiled piece of metal.  4. a place where water flows out of the ground. Read this sentence: 'The rabbit will spring over the log to escape the fox.' Which meaning of 'spring' fits THIS sentence?
choices: a place where water flows out of the ground
questions.hint: Ask which meaning is an ACTION the rabbit does. The word 'over' and 'escape' tell you it is moving, not naming a season or a thing.

questions.prompt: A glossary lists four meanings of 'fair'. Read this sentence: 'It isn't fair that Ben got two cookies and I got none!' Which glossary meaning of 'fair' best matches how it is used here?
choices: treating everyone in an equal way
a fun outdoor event with rides and games
questions.correct: treating everyone in an equal way
questions.hint: Think about WHY the speaker is upset. Which meaning is about people being treated the same, not about weather or a place?

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of word relationships and nuances in word meanings.
standards.parent_tip: Play word games — find synonyms, antonyms, and connect words to real-life experiences.

questions.prompt: Which word means almost the same as **big**?
questions.hint: A synonym is a word with a similar meaning. Which word is similar to 'big'?

questions.prompt: Which word is the OPPOSITE of **hot**?
questions.hint: The opposite of something very warm would be something very...?

questions.prompt: When would you use the word **whisper**?
choices: In a library when you need to be quiet
questions.correct: In a library when you need to be quiet
questions.hint: Whispering means talking very softly. Where would you need to be quiet?

questions.prompt: Which word means the same as **glad**?
questions.hint: 'I'm so glad you're here!' — does that sound happy or sad?

questions.prompt: The puppy was so tired it could barely move. Which word BEST shows how the puppy felt?
questions.hint: Two choices are about being tired, but one is much STRONGER. Match the strength of the word to 'so tired it could barely move.'

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: 'Mom will PARK the car by the store.' Which sentence below uses PARK to mean the SAME thing?
choices: Please park your bike near the wall.
We played all day at the park.
The park has a big slide.
questions.correct: Please park your bike near the wall.
questions.hint: The word PARK can mean a green play space OR to leave something in a spot. Find the sentence where PARK means what it does in 'park the car.'

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the MOST exact word to describe how the wind moved the leaves?
choices: The wind did something to the leaves.
The wind went by the leaves.
The wind swirled the leaves in circles.
questions.correct: The wind swirled the leaves in circles.
questions.hint: Three choices use plain, general words. Pick the one whose word paints the clearest picture of HOW the leaves moved.

questions.prompt: A 'careful' person is full of care. Using that clue, a person who is HELPFUL is a person who:
choices: is full of help for others
questions.correct: is full of help for others
questions.hint: The ending -ful means 'full of.' Careful = full of care. Use that same rule on help + ful.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the child was the MOST happy?
questions.hint: All four are calm-to-excited feelings. Line them up from a little happy to super happy and pick the top.

standards.standard_description: Identify real-life connections between words and their use.
standards.parent_tip: Connect vocabulary to daily life: 'That soup is steaming — what does steaming mean?'

questions.prompt: Which of these foods could be described as **crunchy**?
questions.hint: Think about which food makes a cracking sound when you bite it.

questions.prompt: Which action is **courageous**?
questions.hint: 'Courageous' means brave. Which action takes bravery?

questions.prompt: When might you feel **frustrated**?
choices: When you open a birthday present
When you eat your favorite food
When you play with your best friend
When you can't solve a puzzle after many tries
questions.correct: When you can't solve a puzzle after many tries
questions.hint: Frustrated means feeling stuck or annoyed because something is hard.

questions.prompt: Which place would you describe as **peaceful**?
choices: A quiet garden with birds singing
questions.correct: A quiet garden with birds singing
questions.hint: 'Peaceful' means calm and quiet. Which place sounds calm?

questions.prompt: Which animal would you describe as **swift**?
questions.hint: 'Swift' means very fast. Which animal is the fastest?

questions.prompt: The desert sand was so hot that it burned Mia's bare feet the second she stepped on it. Which word best describes the sand?
questions.hint: The clue is that it BURNED her feet instantly. Pick the word that shows the strongest amount of heat, not just a little heat.

questions.prompt: Which situation would most likely make a person feel FURIOUS?
choices: Dropping your pencil under the desk
Someone breaking your favorite toy on purpose
Finding one dollar on the ground
Waiting a few minutes for lunch
questions.correct: Someone breaking your favorite toy on purpose
questions.hint: Furious means VERY angry, not just a little bothered. Ask which one would make someone the angriest.

questions.prompt: Dad said, "Please turn down the music, it is too loud." What does turn down mean in this sentence?
questions.hint: 'Turn down' can mean different things. Use the words 'too loud' to decide which meaning fits HERE.

questions.prompt: After running a long race in the hot sun, Leo could barely walk and just wanted to lie down. Which word best describes how Leo felt?
questions.hint: He is tired from hard work, not from wanting to sleep and not from having nothing to do. Pick the word for being very worn out.

questions.prompt: The word delicate means something that breaks easily or must be handled gently. Which sentence uses delicate the correct way?
choices: The delicate glass ornament shattered when it fell.
He used the delicate hammer to smash the rocks.
The delicate boulder would not move no matter what.
The delicate truck crashed through the strong wall.
questions.correct: The delicate glass ornament shattered when it fell.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence where the object really is easy to break. Cross out any sentence that shows something strong or heavy.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish shades of meaning among closely related verbs and adjectives.
standards.parent_tip: Discuss word differences: 'Is it the same to toss, throw, and hurl? How are they different?'

questions.prompt: Which word means being MORE than just **warm**?
questions.hint: If warm goes up in temperature, what's the stronger word?

questions.prompt: Which word shows a BIGGER size than **big**?
questions.hint: Big is large. What word means REALLY, REALLY big?

questions.prompt: Which word means walking SLOWLY and tiredly?
questions.hint: Sprinting is fast, marching is steady, skipping is bouncy. Which one is slow and tired?

questions.prompt: The baby was sleeping in her crib. Dad walked in and _____ to his friend so he wouldn't wake her. Which word fits BEST?
questions.hint: All four words mean 'to speak,' but they differ in how LOUD they are. Which one is the quietest? Match it to the sleeping baby.

questions.prompt: Ben had never seen a real dinosaur bone before. He _____ at the giant skeleton for a long, long time without looking away. Which word fits BEST?
questions.hint: Some of these words mean a QUICK look and some mean a LONG look. The clue 'for a long, long time without looking away' tells you which kind you need.

questions.prompt: The school bus was already pulling away, so Lily _____ down the sidewalk to catch it in time. Which word fits BEST?
questions.hint: These are all ways to move on your feet, but they differ in SPEED and purpose. The bus is leaving NOW — how fast would Lily need to go?

questions.prompt: The puppy hadn't eaten all day and was very hungry. When Mom filled its bowl, it _____ the food in just a few seconds. Which word fits BEST?
questions.hint: All four words are about eating, but some mean eating a TINY bit slowly and one means eating a LOT very fast. The clues 'very hungry' and 'in just a few seconds' point the way.

questions.prompt: It was so cold outside that the whole pond had turned to solid ice. Which word BEST describes the air that day?
questions.hint: Three of these words mean some amount of cold, but they aren't equally cold. If the pond froze SOLID, you need the STRONGEST cold word.

standards.standard_description: Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, reading, and being read to, including adjectives and adverbs.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to use descriptive words — 'Don't just say nice, say wonderful, kind, or thoughtful!'

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an adjective to describe the dog?
questions.hint: An adjective describes a noun. Which sentence tells you what KIND of dog?

questions.prompt: The girl sang her favorite song. She wanted all her friends to hear it from far away. Which word best tells how she sang?
questions.hint: Think about what kind of singing helps people hear from far away.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an adverb?
choices: He ran quickly to the bus.
questions.correct: He ran quickly to the bus.
questions.hint: An adverb tells HOW, WHEN, or WHERE. Which sentence has a word that tells HOW?

questions.prompt: Choose the MOST descriptive sentence:
choices: I hungrily ate a warm, cheesy sandwich.
questions.correct: I hungrily ate a warm, cheesy sandwich.
questions.hint: The most descriptive sentence uses adjectives and adverbs to paint a picture!

questions.prompt: The tiny mouse was so scared it would not leave its hole for hours. Which word best describes how the mouse felt?
questions.hint: All four mean a kind of afraid. Pick the STRONGEST one that matches 'so scared.'

questions.prompt: Maria whispered the secret so no one else could hear it. Which sentence uses an adverb that means the OPPOSITE of how Maria spoke?
questions.hint: Three of these mean almost the same as whispering. Find the one that is the reverse.

questions.prompt: Dad said, 'Please turn off the light before bed.' In this sentence, what does the word 'light' mean?
choices: A lamp you switch on and off
questions.correct: A lamp you switch on and off
questions.hint: The word 'light' has more than one meaning. Use the clue 'turn off' to decide which one fits.

questions.prompt: The runner was very thirsty after the long race. She felt as if she had not had a drink in days. Which word best describes the runner?
questions.hint: Which word means VERY dry or thirsty? Watch out for words that mean the opposite.

questions.prompt: A person who is careful never makes messes. So what does it most likely mean if a person is CARELESS?
choices: Making messes and not paying attention
questions.correct: Making messes and not paying attention
questions.hint: The ending -less means 'without.' Add that to the meaning of 'care' to figure it out.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage
standards.parent_tip: Collective nouns (group, team) act as one thing. Irregular verbs like ran, flew just have to be memorized.

questions.prompt: Which sentence shows the correct way to use a verb?
choices: My brother like to read books.
The flowers grows in the garden.
questions.hint: Look carefully at the subject (who or what the sentence is about) and make sure the verb (the action word) matches it.

questions.prompt: Which is the past tense of **fly**?
questions.hint: Fly is irregular. Yesterday it flew!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.hint: Use "I am" for yourself!

questions.prompt: Past tense of **eat**?
questions.hint: Eat is irregular! Yesterday I ate.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The girls fixed the flat tire all by ____." Which word correctly fills the blank?
questions.hint: The doers are more than one girl, so you need the plural reflexive pronoun. Only one of these is real, standard English.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Grandma knitted warm socks for both of my ____." Which word correctly fills the blank?
questions.hint: This noun does not add -s to become plural; it changes its whole shape. Watch out for a plural that has BEEN made plural twice.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The doorbell has ____ three times since we sat down." Which word correctly fills the blank?
questions.hint: The helper word 'has' changes which form of the verb you need. The plain past-tense form is not the one that follows 'has.'

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The old turtle crossed the road very ____." Which word correctly fills the blank?
questions.hint: The blank describes HOW the turtle crossed, so you need the word that describes an action, not a thing.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Of all three backpacks, mine is the ____." Which word correctly fills the blank?
questions.hint: You are comparing three things, not two. Pick the form used when something beats out a whole group.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing
standards.parent_tip: An apostrophe shows ownership (dog's bone) or a contraction (can't). Commas separate items in a list.

questions.prompt: Which shows the dog owns the bone?
questions.hint: Apostrophe + S shows one dog owns it!

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the contraction "can't" correctly?
choices: I cant go to the park today.
I ca'nt go to the park today.
I can't go to the park today.
I can't' go to the park today.
questions.correct: I can't go to the park today.
questions.hint: Remember, the apostrophe in a contraction like "can't" shows where letters are missing when you combine two words.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?
choices: I like apples, bananas, and grapes.
I like apples bananas and grapes.
I, like apples, bananas, and, grapes.
I like, apples, bananas, and grapes.
